Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.front: What is the primary purpose of pre-action protocols?
Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.answer: Pre-action protocols aim to encourage early exchange of information and documents, promote settlement without litigation, and support efficient case management if proceedings are issued. They require parties to act reasonably and proportionately before commencing a claim. The Practice Direction on Pre-Action Conduct and Protocols applies where no specific protocol exists.
Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.summary_line: PRE-ACTION PROTOCOL · EARLY EXCHANGE · SETTLEMENT · PROPORTIONALITY

Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.front: What does the Practice Direction on Pre-Action Conduct require a claimant to do before issuing proceedings?
Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.answer:
Before issuing proceedings, the claimant must:

1. send a letter of claim setting out the basis of the claim, the facts relied on, and the remedy sought
2. allow the defendant a reasonable time to respond
3. consider ADR; and
4. only issue proceedings as a last resort.

Proceedings should not be issued prematurely where a reasonable response time has not expired.
Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.summary_line: LETTER OF CLAIM · REASONABLE TIME · ADR · LAST RESORT

Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.front: What sanctions can a court impose for failure to comply with a pre-action protocol or the Practice Direction on Pre-Action Conduct?
Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.answer: The court may impose costs sanctions, including ordering the non-compliant party to pay costs on an indemnity basis, or disallowing costs. The court may also stay proceedings to allow protocol steps to be completed, or take non-compliance into account when making any costs order. The court expects parties to have acted reasonably and proportionately before commencing litigation.
Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.summary_line: COSTS SANCTIONS · INDEMNITY COSTS · STAY · NON-COMPLIANCE

Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.front: Under the Pre-Action Protocol for Personal Injury Claims, how long does a defendant have to acknowledge a letter of claim, and how long to investigate and respond?
Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.answer:
Under the PI Protocol, the defendant must acknowledge the letter of claim within 21 calendar days. The defendant then has a maximum of 3 months from acknowledgment to investigate the claim and provide a full written response, either admitting liability or giving reasons for denial.

⚠️ Specific timescales vary depending on whether the RTA/EL/PL portal or the full protocol applies.
Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.summary_line: PI PROTOCOL · 21 DAYS ACKNOWLEDGMENT · 3 MONTHS RESPONSE · ADMIT OR DENY

Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.front: What must a letter of claim typically contain?
Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.answer:
A letter of claim should set out:

1. a clear summary of the facts on which the claim is based
2. the legal basis of the claim
3. an indication of the remedy sought (including any financial loss and how it is calculated); and
4. a request for documents relevant to the claim.

It should allow the defendant sufficient time to investigate before proceedings are issued.
Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.summary_line: LETTER OF CLAIM · FACTS · LEGAL BASIS · REMEDY · DOCUMENTS

Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.front: Priya is owed £22,000 by a former business partner. Her solicitor issues proceedings on the day the invoices fall due, without sending a letter of claim. The defendant applies for a costs sanction. What is the likely outcome?
Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.answer: Priya's solicitor has breached the Practice Direction on Pre-Action Conduct. A letter of claim should have been sent, allowing the defendant a reasonable time to respond before proceedings were issued. The court is likely to impose a costs sanction — ordering Priya to pay the defendant's wasted costs, or depriving her of part of her own costs even if she succeeds. The court may also stay proceedings to allow protocol steps to be completed. The invoices being undisputed does not excuse the failure — the protocol exists to allow the defendant to pay voluntarily without the need for proceedings.
Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.summary_line: PRE-ACTION PROTOCOL · LETTER OF CLAIM · PREMATURE ISSUE · COSTS SANCTION · STAY · PROPORTIONALITY

Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.front: Omar sent a letter of claim under the Practice Direction on Pre-Action Conduct on 1 February. The defendant acknowledged receipt on 5 February but has not substantively responded. It is now 1 April. Omar wants to issue proceedings. Can he do so?
Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.answer: Yes. The defendant should be given a reasonable time to respond following the letter of claim. For most non-specialist claims, 14 days is sufficient for a simple matter; more complex claims warrant longer. Here, approximately 7 weeks have elapsed since acknowledgment — well in excess of any reasonable response period. Omar may now issue proceedings. The defendant's failure to engage will be a relevant factor if any future costs application is made against Omar for not following protocol.
Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.summary_line: PRE-ACTION PROTOCOL · REASONABLE RESPONSE TIME · 7 WEEKS · CAN ISSUE · FAILURE TO ENGAGE RELEVANT TO COSTS

Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.front: Ben brings a personal injury claim for a workplace accident. His solicitor sends the letter of claim on 1 March. The defendant's insurer acknowledges it on 10 March. By what date must the insurer provide a substantive response?
Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.answer: Under the Pre-Action Protocol for Personal Injury Claims, the defendant must acknowledge the letter of claim within 21 calendar days — the insurer acknowledged on 10 March, within time. The insurer then has a maximum of 3 months from the date of acknowledgment to investigate and provide a full written response, either admitting or denying liability. The substantive response is therefore due by 10 June at the latest. If no response is received, Ben may issue proceedings.
Pre-Action Protocols & Conduct.summary_line: PI PROTOCOL · 21 DAYS ACKNOWLEDGMENT · 3 MONTHS FROM ACKNOWLEDGMENT · DUE 10 JUNE · ISSUE IF NO RESPONSE

ADR & Settlement.front: What is the court's general attitude towards alternative dispute resolution (ADR) and what are the consequences of unreasonable refusal to engage?
ADR & Settlement.answer:
The court strongly encourages ADR. Under the CPR overriding objective and active case management powers, courts can stay proceedings to allow ADR. A party who unreasonably refuses to engage with ADR may be penalised in costs even if successful at trial: Halsey v Milton Keynes General NHS Trust [2004].

⚠️ Following Churchill v Merthyr Tydfil CBC [2023] (CA), courts may now order parties to participate in ADR in appropriate circumstances — this qualifies the earlier Halsey position on mandatory ADR.
ADR & Settlement.summary_line: ADR · HALSEY · COSTS SANCTIONS · UNREASONABLE REFUSAL · CHURCHILL v MERTHYR TYDFIL [2023]

ADR & Settlement.front: What is a Tomlin order and how does it differ from a consent order?
ADR & Settlement.answer:
A Tomlin order is a court order that stays proceedings on agreed terms that are scheduled to the order (Practice Direction to CPR 40). The stay is permanent unless either party applies to restore the claim. The scheduled terms are not part of the court order itself and are therefore not directly enforceable as a court order — they are enforced as a contract. If one party breaches the scheduled terms, the other may apply to lift the stay and proceed with the original claim, or seek enforcement through the contractual terms.

A Tomlin order differs from a consent order in that: (a) a consent order incorporates the settlement terms into the court order itself, making them directly enforceable by contempt proceedings; (b) a Tomlin order keeps the scheduled terms confidential — they do not appear on the face of the public court record.

⚠️ The confidentiality advantage of a Tomlin order is significant in commercial disputes.
ADR & Settlement.summary_line: TOMLIN ORDER · STAY ON TERMS · SCHEDULE NOT PART OF ORDER · ENFORCED AS CONTRACT · CONFIDENTIAL · CONTRAST CONSENT ORDER

ADR & Settlement.front: Halsey v Milton Keynes General NHS Trust [2004] EWCA Civ 576
ADR & Settlement.issue: Whether a court can compel parties to mediate, and what consequences follow where a party unreasonably refuses to engage in ADR.
ADR & Settlement.rule: A court cannot compel unwilling parties to mediate — to do so would be contrary to Art 6 ECHR (right of access to court). However, a successful party who unreasonably refuses to engage in ADR may be deprived of all or part of their costs, even though they won. In assessing reasonableness, relevant factors include: the nature of the dispute; the merits of the case; whether other settlement methods were attempted; the cost of ADR relative to the cost of litigation; whether ADR would cause delay; and whether it had a reasonable prospect of success.
ADR & Settlement.application: A clinical negligence claim. The defendant NHS Trust refused to mediate. The Court of Appeal held it could not order the parties to mediate, but confirmed that costs sanctions were available for unreasonable refusal. On the facts, the Trust's refusal was not unreasonable given the state of the evidence at the time.
ADR & Settlement.conclusion:
The leading authority on ADR and costs for over two decades.

⚠️ Significantly qualified by Churchill v Merthyr Tydfil CBC [2023] (CA), which held that courts can order stays for ADR (and therefore compel participation indirectly) in appropriate circumstances — the categorical bar on compulsion in Halsey is no longer good law.
ADR & Settlement.summary_line: ADR · UNREASONABLE REFUSAL · COSTS SANCTION · ART 6 ECHR · QUALIFIED BY CHURCHILL [2023]

ADR & Settlement.front: Churchill v Merthyr Tydfil County Borough Council [2023] EWCA Civ 1416
ADR & Settlement.issue: Whether a court has the power to order parties to engage in a non-court-based dispute resolution process, and whether doing so is contrary to Art 6 ECHR.
ADR & Settlement.rule: A court can lawfully stay proceedings to require parties to engage in a non-court-based dispute resolution process (including mediation or an internal complaints procedure), provided the order does not impair the very essence of the claimant's right of access to court. The key conditions are: the ADR process must not be disproportionately expensive, must not cause unacceptable delay, and the court must have the ability to ensure access to court is ultimately preserved. Halsey's suggestion that compulsion is incompatible with Art 6 is not correct.
ADR & Settlement.application: The claimant issued proceedings for damage caused by Japanese knotweed without first using the council's internal complaints procedure. The Court of Appeal held that the County Court had erred in refusing to stay proceedings to allow the complaints procedure to be completed — the court had power to do so and should have exercised it.
ADR & Settlement.conclusion:
A landmark decision qualifying Halsey. Courts can now order mandatory ADR where appropriate. The key limit is that the ADR must not impair the essence of the right of access to court. The decision reflects the CPR's overriding objective and the courts' strong preference for proportionate dispute resolution.

⚠️ The full extent of courts' willingness to order mandatory ADR is still developing post-Churchill.
ADR & Settlement.summary_line: MANDATORY ADR · COURT CAN ORDER STAY · ART 6 NOT ABSOLUTE BAR · QUALIFIES HALSEY · INTERNAL COMPLAINTS PROCEDURE

ADR & Settlement.front: Aisha brings a commercial dispute worth £180,000. The defendant invites her to mediate before trial. Aisha refuses, saying mediation is a waste of time and she is confident of winning. Aisha succeeds at trial and applies for her costs. What costs order is likely?
ADR & Settlement.answer: Aisha is likely to face a costs penalty for unreasonably refusing to engage with mediation. Under Halsey v Milton Keynes General NHS Trust [2004], a successful party who unreasonably refuses ADR may be deprived of all or part of their costs. Confidence in winning is not a reasonable basis for refusal. Following Churchill v Merthyr Tydfil CBC [2023], courts are even more pro-ADR. Aisha will likely receive a reduced costs order, or be ordered to pay some of the defendant's costs despite her success.
ADR & Settlement.summary_line: ADR · HALSEY · UNREASONABLE REFUSAL · COSTS SANCTION · SUCCESS DOES NOT JUSTIFY REFUSAL · CHURCHILL [2023]

ADR & Settlement.front: At the conclusion of a County Court trial, the judge notes that neither party attempted mediation before proceedings were issued. Both parties had exchanged detailed positions and argued mediation was unlikely to succeed given the factual dispute. Does this affect the costs order?
ADR & Settlement.answer: The court may take the failure to attempt ADR into account when making its costs order under CPR 44.2. However, under Halsey, a relevant factor is whether ADR had a reasonable prospect of success. Where both parties had already exchanged detailed positions and the case turned on a fundamental factual dispute unlikely to be resolved by mediation, the court may conclude ADR would not have had a reasonable prospect of success. A blanket failure to even consider ADR will still generally be viewed unfavourably, but on these particular facts a costs sanction is not inevitable.
ADR & Settlement.summary_line: ADR · HALSEY FACTORS · REASONABLE PROSPECT OF SUCCESS · FACTUAL DISPUTE · COURT DISCRETION · CPR 44.2

ADR & Settlement.front: Going deeper: Does Churchill v Merthyr Tydfil CBC [2023] open the door to routinely compelled mediation — and what are the implications for the constitutional right of access to court?
ADR & Settlement.answer:
Churchill qualifies Halsey's position that Art 6 ECHR prevents courts from ordering parties to mediate. The Court of Appeal held courts can order stays for ADR where the process does not impair the very essence of the right of access to court.

This raises a foundational constitutional tension. The right of access to court under Art 6 ECHR and at common law (R v Lord Chancellor, ex p Witham [1997]) has been treated as a constitutional right, subject only to reasonable regulation. Requiring parties to mediate before proceeding to trial is, on one view, a restriction on that access.

Proponents of compelled mediation (Lord Briggs, Civil Courts Structure Review 2016; Civil Justice Council ADR Report 2021) argue the system of court-based adjudication is too expensive for most disputes. Dame Hazel Genn famously argued in 'Mediating Tyranny' (2011) that compelled mediation can itself restrict access to justice if it deprives parties of the right to a reasoned judicial decision — particularly in cases involving public law issues or points of principle where a precedent is needed.

Post-Churchill, the courts have embedded compulsory ADR in the Pre-Action Protocol for Personal Injury Small Claims and the Online Civil Money Claims pilot. Whether this represents a legitimate extension of case management or an erosion of the right to adjudication remains contested.

⚠️ Not SQE-tested: SQE covers Halsey and Churchill at the level of costs consequences.

Jurisdiction & Court Selection.front: In which court should a claim worth £15,000 (non-personal injury) generally be issued?
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.answer: A claim worth £15,000 should generally be issued in the County Court. The County Court has unlimited jurisdiction in contract and tort, but claims should be issued there unless they fall within the High Court's exclusive or concurrent jurisdiction. Claims over £100,000 may be issued in the High Court. A £15,000 claim will typically be allocated to the fast track.

Jurisdiction & Court Selection.front: What is the general financial threshold for issuing a claim in the High Court rather than the County Court?
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.answer: Generally, claims over £100,000 may be issued in the High Court (King's Bench Division). Personal injury claims require a value over £50,000 to be issued in the High Court. Claims of lower value should be issued in the County Court. Some specialist claims (e.g. judicial review, admiralty, certain Commercial Court matters) must be in the High Court regardless of value.
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.summary_line: HIGH COURT · £100,000 · PI £50,000 · COUNTY COURT · SPECIALIST CLAIMS

Jurisdiction & Court Selection.front: What is the limitation period for a claim in contract under the Limitation Act 1980?
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.answer: The limitation period for a claim in contract is 6 years from the date of breach of contract (s5 Limitation Act 1980). For contracts made by deed, the period is 12 years (s8). Time runs from the date of breach, not the date of discovery. The court has no general discretion to extend this period, though specific exceptions exist (e.g. fraud, concealment, or mistake under s32).
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.summary_line: LIMITATION · CONTRACT · 6 YEARS · BREACH · DEED 12 YEARS · s5 LA 1980

Jurisdiction & Court Selection.front: What is the limitation period for a personal injury claim under the Limitation Act 1980?
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.answer: The limitation period for personal injury is 3 years from whichever is the later of: (a) the date on which the cause of action accrued (date of injury); or (b) the date of the claimant's knowledge of the injury, damage, and identity of the defendant (s11 LA 1980). The court also has a discretion to disapply the limitation period under s33 LA 1980 where it would be equitable to do so.
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.summary_line: PI LIMITATION · 3 YEARS · DATE OF KNOWLEDGE · s11 · s33 DISCRETION

Jurisdiction & Court Selection.front: What is the limitation period for a negligence claim involving latent damage (non-personal injury) under the Limitation Act 1980?
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.answer:
For negligence claims involving latent (hidden) damage, the claimant has whichever is the later of: (a) 6 years from accrual of the cause of action; or (b) 3 years from the date of knowledge of the material facts (s14A LA 1980). However, a longstop of 15 years from the date of the negligent act applies regardless of knowledge (s14B LA 1980).

⚠️ This is a frequently tested nuance — distinguish carefully from the standard 6-year tort period. A claimant who discovers damage 12 years after the negligent act is time-barred by the s14B longstop even if they only found out recently.
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.summary_line: LATENT DAMAGE · 6 YEARS OR 3 YEARS FROM KNOWLEDGE · 15-YEAR LONGSTOP · s14A · s14B

Jurisdiction & Court Selection.front: What is the 'date of knowledge' for the purposes of the 3-year limitation period in personal injury claims under s14 Limitation Act 1980?
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.summary_line: DATE OF KNOWLEDGE · s14 LA 1980 · SIGNIFICANT INJURY · ATTRIBUTABLE · IDENTITY OF DEFENDANT · CONSTRUCTIVE KNOWLEDGE s14(3) · FACTUAL NOT LEGAL

Jurisdiction & Court Selection.front: Claudia breached a contract with Tom on 14 March 2018. Tom takes no steps to pursue the claim. On 15 March 2024, Tom issues proceedings. Is his claim in time?
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.answer: No. The limitation period for contract is 6 years from the date of breach (s5 Limitation Act 1980). Tom's cause of action accrued on 14 March 2018. Six years expires on 14 March 2024. Tom's claim was issued on 15 March 2024 — one day after the limitation period expired. The claim is time-barred. There is no general discretion to extend the limitation period in contract claims.
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.summary_line: LIMITATION · CONTRACT · s5 LA 1980 · 6 YEARS · LAST DAY 14 MARCH 2024 · ISSUED 15 MARCH 2024 · OUT OF TIME

Jurisdiction & Court Selection.front: Maya suffered an injury at work on 1 June 2020. She did not realise the injury might be work-related until told by her doctor on 1 December 2021. She issues proceedings on 1 November 2024. Is her claim in time?
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.answer: Yes. Under s11 Limitation Act 1980, the 3-year period runs from the later of: (a) the date of injury (1 June 2020); or (b) the date of knowledge (1 December 2021). The date of knowledge is later, so time runs from 1 December 2021. Three years from that date is 1 December 2024. Maya issued on 1 November 2024 — within the limitation period. The date of knowledge starts the clock, provided the claimant did not have constructive knowledge earlier.
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.summary_line: PI LIMITATION · s11 LA 1980 · DATE OF KNOWLEDGE 1 DEC 2021 · EXPIRES 1 DEC 2024 · ISSUED 1 NOV 2024 · IN TIME

Jurisdiction & Court Selection.front: A surveyor negligently failed to identify structural defects in a report in March 2010. The defects only became apparent in February 2022. The purchaser issues proceedings in January 2025. Advise on limitation.
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.answer:
This is a latent damage claim in negligence governed by ss14A and 14B Limitation Act 1980. The purchaser has whichever is the later of: (a) 6 years from accrual (date of purchase, approximately 2010) — expired around 2016; or (b) 3 years from the date of knowledge (February 2022) — expires February 2025. Proceedings were issued in January 2025, within limb (b). However, the s14B 15-year longstop runs from the negligent act (March 2010) and expires March 2025. On these facts the claim is just within time.

⚠️ If proceedings had been issued after March 2025, the longstop would have barred them regardless of when damage was discovered.
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.summary_line: LATENT DAMAGE · s14A LA 1980 · 3 YEARS FROM KNOWLEDGE FEB 2022 · EXPIRES FEB 2025 · s14B LONGSTOP MARCH 2025 · IN TIME

Jurisdiction & Court Selection.front: Reena has a negligence claim worth approximately £90,000 for property damage. Her solicitor wants to issue in the High Court. Is this appropriate?
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.answer:
A non-PI claim worth £90,000 falls below the general £100,000 threshold for issue in the High Court (KBD). It is not a PI claim (which has a lower £50,000 threshold), nor is it a specialist claim requiring the High Court. The claim should be issued in the County Court. Issuing in the High Court at £90,000 would be inappropriate and the court may transfer it down to the County Court. Once in the County Court it will be allocated to the multi-track (above £25,000).

⚠️ The £100,000 threshold is for non-PI claims — £90,000 falls short.
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.summary_line: HIGH COURT THRESHOLD · £100,000 NON-PI · £90,000 BELOW THRESHOLD · ISSUE IN COUNTY COURT · MULTI-TRACK · TRANSFER DOWN

Jurisdiction & Court Selection.front: David suffered a serious injury at work in January 2020. His solicitor advises the limitation period expired in January 2023. It is now May 2025. Can he still bring the claim?
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.answer:
The primary limitation period under s11 LA 1980 expired in January 2023. David may apply under s33 LA 1980 for the court to exercise its discretion to disapply the limitation period where equitable to do so. The court considers:

1. length and reason for delay
2. effect on cogency of evidence
3. conduct of the defendant
4. degree of disability of the claimant
5. promptness once facts were known; and
6. steps taken to obtain advice.

David must positively establish it is equitable to disapply. There is no guarantee — the longer and less explicable the delay, the harder a s33 order is to obtain.
Jurisdiction & Court Selection.summary_line: PI LIMITATION · s11 EXPIRED JAN 2023 · s33 LA 1980 DISCRETION · SIX FACTORS · CLAIMANT MUST ESTABLISH EQUITABLE · NO GUARANTEE

Claim Form & Service.front: Once a claim form has been issued, within what period must it be served on the defendant?
Claim Form & Service.answer: A claim form must be served within 4 months of the date of issue (CPR 7.5). Where the claim form is to be served out of the jurisdiction, the period is 6 months. The court may extend time for service in exceptional circumstances, but claimants should not assume extensions will be granted as the courts apply a strict approach.
Claim Form & Service.summary_line: SERVICE · CLAIM FORM · 4 MONTHS · 6 MONTHS OVERSEAS · CPR 7.5

Claim Form & Service.front: What are the permitted methods of service of a claim form on an individual in England and Wales?
Claim Form & Service.answer:
Under CPR 6.3, permitted methods of service include:

1. personal service
2. first class post, document exchange, or other service providing for next-day delivery
3. leaving the document at a specified place
4. fax or other electronic means (where the party has confirmed willingness to accept in writing); and
5. any other method authorised by the court.

Service by email requires prior written agreement or court permission.
Claim Form & Service.summary_line: SERVICE METHODS · PERSONAL · POST · DX · FAX · ELECTRONIC · CPR 6.3

Claim Form & Service.front: When is a document deemed served when sent by first class post, and why does it matter?
Claim Form & Service.answer:
Under the deemed service table at CPR 6.26, a document sent by first class post is deemed served on the second business day after posting. 'Business day' means any day except Saturday, Sunday, a bank holiday, Christmas Day, or Good Friday. For example, a claim form posted on a Monday is deemed served on Wednesday.

This matters because time for filing an acknowledgment of service (14 days) and a defence (14 or 28 days) runs from the deemed service date, not the date of actual receipt.

⚠️ Students frequently miscalculate service dates by treating calendar days as business days, or by counting from the posting date rather than the day after.
Claim Form & Service.summary_line: DEEMED SERVICE · FIRST CLASS POST · SECOND BUSINESS DAY · CPR 6.26 · AOS AND DEFENCE DEADLINES

Claim Form & Service.front: A claim form is issued on 3 March 2026. The claimant's solicitor posts it by first class post on 2 July 2026. When is it deemed served and is it in time?
Claim Form & Service.answer:
The claim form must be served within 4 months of issue — by 3 July 2026 (CPR 7.5). The claim form was posted on 2 July. Under CPR 6.26, a document sent by first class post is deemed served on the second business day after posting. Assuming 2 July is a Thursday, deemed service falls on Monday 6 July — after the 4-month deadline. The claim form has been served out of time. The claimant would need to apply under CPR 7.6 to extend the service period, which requires exceptional circumstances and is frequently refused.

⚠️ Posting on the last day of the service window does not satisfy the deadline — the deemed service date must fall within the period.
Claim Form & Service.summary_line: SERVICE · CLAIM FORM · 4 MONTHS BY 3 JULY · POSTED 2 JULY · DEEMED SERVED 6 JULY · OUT OF TIME · CPR 7.6 EXTENSION DIFFICULT

Claim Form & Service.front: A solicitor serves the claim form by emailing it to the defendant's known email address. The defendant had never indicated willingness to accept service by email. The defendant objects. Is the objection well-founded?
Claim Form & Service.answer: Yes. Under CPR 6.3, service by electronic means (including email) is only a permitted method where the party to be served has indicated in writing their willingness to accept service by that method. Mere possession of an email address is not such an indication. The claim form has not been validly served. The claimant must re-serve by a permitted method within the 4-month service period. If that period has now expired, the claimant must apply to extend time under CPR 7.6, which requires exceptional circumstances.
Claim Form & Service.summary_line: SERVICE · EMAIL · PRIOR WRITTEN AGREEMENT REQUIRED · CPR 6.3 · NOT VALID SERVICE · RE-SERVE WITHIN 4 MONTHS · CPR 7.6 IF EXPIRED

Claim Form & Service.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A claim form and particulars of claim are sent by first class post on Monday 9 March 2026. The claimant's solicitor tells the client that the defendant must file an acknowledgment of service by Monday 23 March — 14 days from the date of posting.
Claim Form & Service.issue: The Trap: The obvious conclusion is that the 14-day period for the acknowledgment of service starts running from the date the documents were posted (9 March), giving a deadline of 23 March. Students know service by post is valid and that the period is 14 days — they apply those rules but forget the deemed service calculation.
Claim Form & Service.rule: The Reality: Under CPR 6.26, first class post is deemed served on the second business day after posting. Posted Monday 9 March → deemed served Wednesday 11 March. The 14-day period for filing an acknowledgment of service (CPR 10.3) runs from deemed service: 14 days from 11 March = Wednesday 25 March 2026. The deadline is 25 March — not 23 March.
Claim Form & Service.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is the phrase 'received on Thursday'. Students read 'received' and calculate 14 days from receipt. But CPR 6.26 deems service on the second business day after posting — regardless of when the document actually arrives. Receipt is irrelevant to the deemed service calculation.
Claim Form & Service.conclusion: The Key Rule: The 14-day period in CPR 10.3 runs from the deemed service date (second business day after posting), not from the date of posting or actual receipt. The deemed service date is always later than the posting date, giving the defendant more time than the claimant calculated.
Claim Form & Service.summary_line: DEEMED SERVICE · SECOND BUSINESS DAY AFTER POSTING · AOS DEADLINE RUNS FROM DEEMED SERVICE · CPR 6.26 · CPR 10.3

Particulars of Claim.front: What must particulars of claim contain, and when must they be served?
Particulars of Claim.answer:
Particulars of claim must contain (CPR 16.4):

1. a concise statement of the facts on which the claimant relies
2. the remedy sought
3. where interest is claimed, the basis (contractual or statutory under s69 County Courts Act 1984 / s35A Senior Courts Act 1981) and the daily rate
4. a statement of value where required.

Where aggravated, exemplary, or provisional damages are sought, this must be specifically stated.

Particulars of claim may be served with the claim form or, if not included, within 14 days after service of the claim form (CPR 7.4). They must in any event be served within the claim form's 4-month service period.
Particulars of Claim.summary_line: PARTICULARS OF CLAIM · CONCISE FACTS · REMEDY · INTEREST · 14 DAYS AFTER SERVICE · CPR 16.4

Defence & Default.front: What must a defence contain and what is the consequence of failing to address an allegation?
Defence & Default.answer:
A defence must state which allegations in the particulars of claim are: (a) denied (with reasons); (b) admitted; or (c) neither admitted nor denied (with reasons) (CPR 16.5).

Critical rule: where a defendant fails to deal with an allegation in the particulars of claim, that allegation is treated as admitted (CPR 16.5(5)). This deemed admission rule applies even if the omission is inadvertent.

⚠️ The deemed admission rule is a key exam trap. A defendant who omits to address a claim on quantum — even if they deny liability — is taken to have admitted the amount claimed.
Defence & Default.summary_line: DEFENCE · DENY/ADMIT/NOT ADMIT · DEEMED ADMISSION · CPR 16.5(5) · INADVERTENT OMISSION

Defence & Default.front: What is an acknowledgment of service, when must it be filed, and what is its effect on the defence deadline?
Defence & Default.answer:
An acknowledgment of service (AoS) is a form filed by the defendant to signal receipt of the claim and an intention to defend, contest jurisdiction, or seek more time. It must be filed within 14 days of service of the particulars of claim (CPR 10.3).

Filing an AoS extends the deadline for filing a defence from 14 days to 28 days from service of the particulars of claim. The AoS does not itself constitute a defence — the defendant must still file a full defence within the extended period.

If neither an AoS nor a defence is filed, the claimant may apply for default judgment under CPR 12.
Defence & Default.summary_line: AOS · 14 DAYS TO FILE · EXTENDS DEFENCE TO 28 DAYS · DEFAULT JUDGMENT IF NONE FILED · CPR 10.3

Defence & Default.front: When may default judgment be obtained, and on what grounds may it be set aside?
Defence & Default.answer:
Default judgment may be obtained where the defendant has failed to file an AoS or defence within the required period (CPR 12.3). It is available for money claims, delivery of goods, and other specified relief.

Default judgment may be set aside under CPR 13 on two bases:

1. As of right: where judgment was wrongly entered (e.g. filed before the deadline expired, or the defendant had already satisfied the claim).

2. Discretionary: where the defendant shows a real prospect of successfully defending the claim, or there is some other good reason to set aside or vary the judgment.

The promptness of the application is a relevant factor.

⚠️ The as-of-right / discretionary distinction is a frequent exam trap. Only in the discretionary category must the defendant show a real prospect of success.
Defence & Default.summary_line: DEFAULT JUDGMENT · CPR 12.3 · SET ASIDE AS OF RIGHT · DISCRETIONARY: REAL PROSPECT · PROMPTNESS · CPR 13

Defence & Default.front: A claim form and particulars of claim are served on Monday 2 March 2026 by first class post. No acknowledgment of service is filed. When does the defence deadline expire and what may the claimant do if no defence is filed?
Defence & Default.answer:
Particulars sent by first class post are deemed served on the second business day after posting — Wednesday 4 March 2026 (CPR 6.26). Where no acknowledgment of service is filed, the defence must be filed within 14 days of service of the particulars: by Wednesday 18 March 2026. If the defendant files neither an AoS nor a defence by that date, the claimant may apply for default judgment under CPR 12.

⚠️ The 14-day period runs from deemed service, not from the date of posting or actual receipt.
Defence & Default.summary_line: DEEMED SERVICE · SECOND BUSINESS DAY · 4 MARCH · DEFENCE BY 18 MARCH · NO AOS = 14 DAYS · DEFAULT JUDGMENT CPR 12

Defence & Default.front: Particulars of claim are served on James on 1 April. James files an acknowledgment of service on 10 April. His solicitors then fail to file a defence. The claimant applies for default judgment. James's solicitor argues that because James has a solid defence, default judgment should be refused. Advise.
Defence & Default.answer: Default judgment may be entered under CPR 12.3 — it does not require consideration of the merits at the point of entry. Once entered, James may apply to set it aside under CPR 13. On these facts this would be discretionary — James must show a real prospect of successfully defending the claim and/or some other good reason. The promptness of the application is also relevant. James has a good argument on the merits, but those merits are irrelevant at the point of entry of default judgment — they are only considered on a subsequent set-aside application.
Defence & Default.summary_line: DEFAULT JUDGMENT · CPR 12.3 · AOS → 28 DAYS · MERITS IRRELEVANT AT ENTRY · SET ASIDE CPR 13 · DISCRETIONARY: REAL PROSPECT

Defence & Default.front: Default judgment is entered against Nadia. Her solicitor argues it should be set aside as of right because Nadia had already sent a letter before the deadline disputing the entire claim. Advise.
Defence & Default.answer: Set aside as of right under CPR 13.2 applies only where judgment was wrongly entered — for example, where judgment was entered before the defence deadline had expired, or where the defendant had already satisfied the claim. Nadia's letter disputing the claim does not constitute an acknowledgment of service or a defence filed at court. Unless the letter was formally filed at court within the required period, it does not prevent default judgment being entered. Set aside as of right is therefore not available. Nadia must rely on the discretionary ground in CPR 13.3 — showing a real prospect of success on the defence and explaining any delay.
Defence & Default.summary_line: DEFAULT JUDGMENT SET ASIDE · CPR 13.2 AS OF RIGHT: WRONGLY ENTERED ONLY · LETTER NOT AOS · CPR 13.3 DISCRETIONARY: REAL PROSPECT

Defence & Default.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Jake's defence denies liability for the car accident in full, with detailed reasons. His solicitors address every element of the negligence claim. However, the defence says nothing about the claimant's claim for £24,000 in vehicle repair costs. Jake's solicitor says this doesn't matter because the whole claim has been denied.
Defence & Default.issue: The Trap: Students think a general denial of liability covers all claims arising from the incident, including quantum. If Jake has denied the accident was his fault, logically he is also denying the loss. The defence is detailed and professional — it does not look like an omission.
Defence & Default.rule: The Reality: Under CPR 16.5(5), if a defendant fails to deal with an allegation in the particulars of claim, that allegation is deemed to be admitted. The £24,000 vehicle repair claim is a separate allegation that Jake's defence does not address. The deemed admission rule operates automatically — it does not matter that Jake denied liability in general terms. Jake is taken to have admitted that vehicle repair costs of £24,000 are owed.
Defence & Default.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is the general denial of liability, which feels comprehensive. Students assume that if you deny causing the accident you must also be denying the loss. But CPR 16.5(5) requires each allegation to be addressed specifically. Quantum allegations are separate and are admitted by default if not denied.
Defence & Default.conclusion: The Key Rule: Denying liability does not automatically deny quantum. Every allegation in the particulars of claim must be specifically addressed in the defence. A defendant who denies liability but says nothing about the claimant's quantification of loss is deemed to have admitted the amount claimed.
Defence & Default.summary_line: DEEMED ADMISSION · CPR 16.5(5) · MUST ADDRESS EVERY ALLEGATION · LIABILITY DENIAL ≠ QUANTUM DENIAL · INADVERTENT OMISSION FATAL

Defence & Default.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Default judgment is entered against Sofia after her defence deadline expires. Sofia's solicitor immediately applies to set aside the judgment. Sofia has strong evidence that she filed the defence in time — she has a PDF of the filed document and the filing receipt. Her solicitor argues she must show a real prospect of success.
Defence & Default.issue: The Trap: The scenario involves a set-aside application, and students know the test from CPR 13. The first thing most students reach for is the discretionary test in CPR 13.3 — real prospect of successfully defending the claim — because it is better known and more commonly applied.
Defence & Default.rule: The Reality: Under CPR 13.2, default judgment must be set aside as of right where it was wrongly entered. If Sofia has evidence that her defence was filed before the deadline — the PDF and filing receipt — then the default judgment was entered when a defence was already on the court file. It was wrongly entered. Set aside is mandatory, not discretionary. Sofia does not need to show a real prospect of success at all.
Defence & Default.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is the word 'set-aside', which triggers the CPR 13.3 discretionary test students have memorised. But CPR 13.2 creates a mandatory right to set aside where default judgment was wrongly entered — the discretionary test never arises. Always check CPR 13.2 before reaching CPR 13.3.
Defence & Default.conclusion: The Key Rule: Before applying the discretionary CPR 13.3 test, always check whether default judgment was wrongly entered (CPR 13.2). If judgment was entered in error — because a defence was already filed or the period had not yet expired — set aside is as of right. The real prospect of success test only applies to discretionary set aside.
Defence & Default.summary_line: DEFAULT JUDGMENT · SET ASIDE AS OF RIGHT: CPR 13.2 · WRONGLY ENTERED · REAL PROSPECT ONLY FOR DISCRETIONARY: CPR 13.3 · CHECK AS OF RIGHT FIRST

Amendments.front: When may a party amend a statement of case without permission, and what restrictions apply where limitation has expired?
Amendments.answer:
Under CPR 17, a party may amend a statement of case:

1. Without permission: once at any time before service of the defence (or AoS); or with the written consent of all parties.

2. With the court's permission: in all other cases.

Permission is given if the amendment is arguable and any prejudice can be compensated in costs.

Where limitation has expired, the court may only allow an amendment that adds or substitutes a new cause of action if the new claim arises out of the same or substantially the same facts as the existing claim (CPR 17.4, s35 Limitation Act 1980).

⚠️ Post-limitation amendments adding new causes of action are strictly controlled — the court does not exercise a general discretion; it applies the same-facts test as a threshold condition.
Amendments.summary_line: AMENDMENT · CPR 17 · ONCE WITHOUT PERMISSION · POST-LIMITATION s35 LA 1980 · SAME FACTS TEST

Amendments.front: Sarah's particulars of claim allege breach of an oral contract in May 2020. Two years into proceedings, after limitation has expired, her solicitor seeks to amend to add a claim for negligent misstatement arising from the same pre-contractual negotiations. Should permission be granted?
Amendments.answer: Sarah requires the court's permission to amend after a defence has been filed (CPR 17). Since limitation has expired, the court can only allow an amendment adding a new cause of action if the new claim arises out of the same or substantially the same facts as already pleaded (CPR 17.4, s35 Limitation Act 1980). Here, the negligent misstatement claim arises from the same pre-contractual negotiations — same parties, time period, conversations. The new claim likely arises from substantially the same facts and permission is likely to be granted, subject to any prejudice to the defendant not compensable in costs. The same-facts test is a threshold condition, not a broad discretion.
Amendments.summary_line: AMENDMENT · CPR 17 · POST-LIMITATION s35 LA 1980 · SAME FACTS TEST · SUBSTANTIALLY SAME FACTS · PERMISSION LIKELY

Part 20 Claims.front: What is a Part 20 claim and what are the procedural requirements for making one?
Part 20 Claims.answer:
Part 20 of the CPR covers additional claims, which include:

1. Counterclaims: a claim by the defendant against the claimant. Filed with the defence. Treated as if it were a separate claim for most purposes, including costs. The defendant does not need permission to counterclaim if filed with the defence; permission is required if filed later.

2. Additional claims against third parties: the defendant brings a new claim against someone not already a party (CPR 20.7), for example for contribution or indemnity. Permission from the court is required after the defence has been filed unless all parties consent.

3. Claims against existing parties: a defendant claims against a co-defendant.

A Part 20 claim is served with the statement of case unless the court gives other directions. The third party has the same rights as a defendant to file a defence and counterclaim.

⚠️ Costs of a counterclaim are treated separately from the main claim — a claimant who wins the main claim but loses the counterclaim may face a net adverse costs position.
Part 20 Claims.summary_line: PART 20 · COUNTERCLAIM · ADDITIONAL CLAIM AGAINST THIRD PARTY · CONTRIBUTION/INDEMNITY · NO PERMISSION IF WITH DEFENCE · SEPARATE COSTS

Part 20 Claims.front: Tom sues his builder for £30,000 for defective work. The builder files a defence and counterclaim for £15,000 for unpaid invoices on the same day. At trial, Tom wins on the main claim and the builder wins on the counterclaim. Advise on costs.
Part 20 Claims.answer:
The main claim and counterclaim are treated as separate proceedings for costs purposes under Part 20. Each party is prima facie entitled to costs on their respective successful claim under CPR 44.2. The court is likely to make a net order: Tom receives costs on the main claim; the builder receives costs on the counterclaim. The court may also consider a single percentage order reflecting overall success. Tom cannot claim to have won 'overall' simply because his claim exceeded the counterclaim in value — each claim is assessed separately.

⚠️ The builder's costs on the counterclaim reduce Tom's net recovery.
Part 20 Claims.summary_line: PART 20 · COUNTERCLAIM SEPARATE CLAIM · COSTS TREATED SEPARATELY · NET COSTS ORDER · EACH CLAIM ASSESSED SEPARATELY

Reply to Defence.front: What is a reply to defence, when must it be served, and what is the consequence of not serving one?
Reply to Defence.answer:
A reply to defence is served by the claimant where they wish to allege facts in answer to the defence that are not already dealt with in the particulars of claim (CPR 15.8). It must be served at the same time as the directions questionnaire. A reply is not compulsory — it is only required where the claimant wishes to respond to a specific allegation in the defence.

If no reply is served, the claimant is taken to join issue on the matters raised in the defence — in effect, all facts in the defence are treated as disputed (CPR 16.7(1)). The claimant does not need to serve a reply simply to deny the defence.

⚠️ A reply cannot be used to introduce a new cause of action or to raise a claim not already pleaded in the particulars of claim — any new cause of action requires an amendment to the particulars under CPR 17.

⚠️ Link — Amendments (see FLK1-DR-059): adding a new cause of action requires permission and must satisfy the CPR 17.4 same-facts test if limitation has expired.
Reply to Defence.summary_line: REPLY TO DEFENCE · CPR 15.8 · NOT COMPULSORY · JOIN ISSUE IF NO REPLY · CPR 16.7(1) · CANNOT INTRODUCE NEW CLAIM · DISTINGUISH AMENDMENT CPR 17

Track Allocation.front: What are the three case management tracks and what are the financial thresholds for each?
Track Allocation.summary_line: SMALL CLAIMS <=£10,000 · FAST TRACK £10,000-£25,000 · MULTI-TRACK £25,000+ · PI LOWER LIMITS · CPR 26

Track Allocation.front: What are the key procedural features of the small claims track?
Track Allocation.answer:
Key features of the small claims track (CPR 27):

1. Costs: legal costs are generally not recoverable. Each party bears their own legal costs save for court fees and limited fixed costs (the 'no costs' rule, CPR 27.14).

2. Hearings are informal; strict rules of evidence do not apply.

3. Paper determination is possible for straightforward claims.

4. Expert evidence requires permission and the court gives tight directions.

5. Lay representatives may appear on a party's behalf.

⚠️ The no-costs rule is the most exam-tested feature of the small claims track. A winning claimant cannot recover solicitors' fees — this is the primary reason parties may prefer a higher-value claim to be allocated upward.
Track Allocation.summary_line: SMALL CLAIMS · NO COSTS RULE · CPR 27.14 · INFORMAL HEARING · LAY REPRESENTATIVES · PAPER DETERMINATION

Track Allocation.front: What are the key features and standard timetable on the fast track?
Track Allocation.summary_line: FAST TRACK · ONE-DAY TRIAL · STANDARD DIRECTIONS · SJE · FIXED TRIAL COSTS · 30 WEEKS TO TRIAL · CPR 28

Track Allocation.front: What are the key case management tools used on the multi-track?
Track Allocation.summary_line: MULTI-TRACK · CMC · PTR · COSTS BUDGETING · CCMC · PRECEDENT H · TAILORED DIRECTIONS · CPR 29

Track Allocation.front: What factors does the court consider when allocating a case to a track?
Track Allocation.answer:
Under CPR 26.8, the court considers:

1. the financial value of the claim (after disregarding any amount not in dispute)
2. the nature of the remedy sought
3. the likely complexity of facts, law, or evidence
4. the number of parties
5. the value of any counterclaim
6. the amount of oral evidence required
7. the importance of the claim to non-parties or public interest; and
8. the parties' views and circumstances.

Financial value is the starting point but is not conclusive. A low-value case with complex legal issues may be allocated to a higher track. A claimant may deliberately undervalue a claim to obtain small claims allocation (no-costs environment), but the court may reallocate.
Track Allocation.summary_line: TRACK ALLOCATION · CPR 26.8 · FINANCIAL VALUE · COMPLEXITY · NUMBER OF PARTIES · COURT OVERRIDES VALUE

Track Allocation.front: What is a directions questionnaire and what information must it contain?
Track Allocation.answer:
A directions questionnaire (DQ) is filed by each party after a defence is filed, triggering the court's allocation of the case to a track (CPR 26). The forms are N180 (small claims/fast track) and N181 (multi-track). The DQ must address:

1. whether the party proposes a particular track
2. compliance with pre-action protocols
3. whether ADR has been considered or attempted
4. proposed directions and timetable
5. costs estimate (fast track and multi-track).

The court uses the DQ to make an allocation decision. A party who fails to file a DQ risks the court making an order on its own initiative or striking out the claim or defence.
Track Allocation.summary_line: DIRECTIONS QUESTIONNAIRE · N180/N181 · TRACK PROPOSAL · ADR · COSTS ESTIMATE · TRIGGERS ALLOCATION · CPR 26

Track Allocation.front: What is the pre-trial checklist (listing questionnaire), what must it contain, and what are the consequences of failing to file it?
Track Allocation.answer:
The pre-trial checklist (Form N170, also called the listing questionnaire) is filed by each party at the pre-trial checklist stage, after the main directions have been complied with (CPR 28.5 fast track; CPR 29.6 multi-track). It confirms the parties' readiness for trial and must include:

1. Confirmation that directions have been complied with

2. Details of witnesses to be called and their availability

3. Expert evidence and whether experts have met

4. Estimate of trial length and any revised costs estimate

5. Whether a pre-trial review is sought

The court uses the checklist to fix the trial date or confirm a listing window.

Failure to file may result in the court striking out the claim or defence, or fixing a hearing to determine the appropriate sanction.

⚠️ Distinguish from the directions questionnaire (DQ), which triggers initial allocation: the pre-trial checklist confirms readiness at the later pre-trial stage and feeds directly into the trial listing.
Track Allocation.summary_line: PRE-TRIAL CHECKLIST · N170 · CPR 28.5 / 29.6 · TRIAL READINESS · WITNESSES · EXPERTS · TRIAL ESTIMATE · STRIKE OUT IF FAILED · DISTINGUISH DQ

Track Allocation.front: Elaine has a personal injury claim worth £9,000 from a road traffic accident and wants her solicitor to represent her and recover fees if she wins. Advise on track allocation.
Track Allocation.answer: A personal injury claim worth £9,000 is allocated to the fast track. The small claims track threshold for PI claims is £1,000 — claims above £1,000 go to the fast track (up to £25,000). On the fast track, legal costs are recoverable if Elaine wins, subject to fixed recoverable costs. This is a significant advantage compared to a non-PI claim worth £9,000, which would be in small claims with no recoverable legal costs. Elaine should be advised that the PI nature of her claim means fast track allocation, making representation cost-effective if she succeeds.
Track Allocation.summary_line: PI CLAIM £9,000 · FAST TRACK (PI THRESHOLD FROM £1,000) · FIXED RECOVERABLE COSTS · NOT SMALL CLAIMS · DISTINGUISH NON-PI

Track Allocation.front: A breach of contract dispute worth £18,000 raises novel and complex issues of construction law never previously decided by the courts. The claimant argues fast track; the defendant argues multi-track. Advise.
Track Allocation.answer: Track allocation is determined by all circumstances under CPR 26.8, not financial value alone. While a claim worth £18,000 would normally be allocated to the fast track, the court will depart from this where the case is too complex for a one-day fast track trial. Novel and complex legal issues of construction law strongly suggest multi-track allocation, which allows a CMC, tailored directions, and a longer trial. The defendant's argument is well-founded. The claimant may prefer the fast track's fixed costs regime but complexity is explicitly one of the CPR 26.8 factors and is likely to prevail here.
Track Allocation.summary_line: TRACK ALLOCATION · CPR 26.8 · £18,000 = FAST TRACK DEFAULT · COMPLEXITY OVERRIDES · NOVEL LEGAL ISSUES · MULTI-TRACK APPROPRIATE

Track Allocation.front: A consumer brings a claim for £7,500 for faulty building work. The defendant argues the work was correct and pre-existing defects are to blame. Expert surveyor evidence is required. The claimant's solicitor wants small claims to avoid adverse costs. Advise.
Track Allocation.answer: The claim is within the small claims threshold (up to £10,000 for non-PI). However, the court may allocate to the fast track if the case is too complex for a small claims hearing — the need for expert surveyor evidence is relevant under CPR 26.8. The no-costs rule in CPR 27.14 operates both ways: if small claims, the claimant cannot recover solicitor fees even if they win, but if they lose, the defendant equally cannot recover legal costs. The court may allocate upward given the expert evidence. The claimant's solicitor should advise the client that small claims protects against adverse legal costs but also prevents recovering their own solicitor fees.
Track Allocation.summary_line: SMALL CLAIMS · £7,500 · NO COSTS RULE BOTH WAYS · EXPERT EVIDENCE MAY PUSH TO FAST TRACK · CPR 26.8 DISCRETION · PROTECTION AGAINST ADVERSE COSTS

Track Allocation.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A homeowner brings a claim for £8,500 for defective kitchen installation. The case is allocated to the small claims track. The solicitor tells the client that because she has a strong case and is almost certain to win, she should instruct the solicitor to represent her and the costs will be recovered from the defendant if she wins.
Track Allocation.issue: The Trap: The advice sounds reasonable: you win a case, you recover costs. The general rule under CPR 44.2 is that the winning party recovers their costs from the losing party. Students apply the general costs rule without recognising that the small claims track disapplies it entirely.
Track Allocation.rule: The Reality: On the small claims track, the no-costs rule in CPR 27.14 means that solicitor fees (legal costs) are not recoverable from the other side, regardless of the outcome. Even if the homeowner wins, the defendant will not be ordered to pay her solicitor's fees. The only costs recoverable on small claims are: the court issue fee; limited fixed costs in certain circumstances; and out-of-pocket expenses.
Track Allocation.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is the costs-follow-the-event rule (CPR 44.2), which students correctly recall. They apply it to the successful claimant without checking the track allocation. CPR 27.14 disapplies the general costs rule on the small claims track — solicitor costs are not recoverable regardless of outcome.
Track Allocation.conclusion: The Key Rule: The small claims track no-costs rule (CPR 27.14) disapplies the general CPR 44.2 'loser pays' rule for legal costs. Solicitor fees are not recoverable on the small claims track regardless of outcome. This is the most important costs feature of the small claims track and the most common source of client surprises.
Track Allocation.summary_line: SMALL CLAIMS · NO COSTS RULE · CPR 27.14 · SOLICITOR FEES NOT RECOVERABLE · REGARDLESS OF OUTCOME · ONLY COURT FEES AND FIXED COSTS

Overriding Objective.front: What is the overriding objective of the CPR and what duty does it impose on the parties?
Overriding Objective.answer:
The overriding objective (CPR 1.1) is to enable the court to deal with cases justly and at proportionate cost. Dealing justly includes:

1. ensuring the parties are on an equal footing and can participate fully
2. saving expense
3. dealing with the case in ways that are proportionate to the amount, importance, complexity, and the financial position of each party
4. ensuring it is dealt with expeditiously and fairly; and
5. allotting to it an appropriate share of the court's resources.

Parties have a positive duty to help the court further the overriding objective (CPR 1.3). The court must seek to give effect to it whenever it exercises any power under the CPR or interprets any rule.
Overriding Objective.summary_line: OVERRIDING OBJECTIVE · CPR 1.1 · JUSTLY AND PROPORTIONATE COST · EQUAL FOOTING · PARTIES' DUTY CPR 1.3

Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.front: What is the Denton test for relief from sanctions and how is it applied?
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.answer:
Following Mitchell v News Group Newspapers Ltd [2013] and Denton v TH White Ltd [2014] (CA), the court applies CPR 3.9 via a three-stage test:

1. How serious and significant was the breach? Trivial breaches may be relieved without detailed analysis of the other stages.

2. What was the reason for the breach? A good reason (e.g. illness, accident) may justify relief; no good reason or a weak one weighs against relief.

3. Evaluate all the circumstances, including: the need to conduct litigation efficiently and at proportionate cost; the need to enforce compliance with rules and orders; and all other relevant matters (including whether the other party has been prejudiced).

⚠️ Courts apply a firm approach — even where the breach is non-trivial, relief is not automatic. Delay in applying for relief is itself a factor against granting it.

⚠️ Link — Unless Orders (see FLK1-DR-058): where the sanction is imposed by an unless order, the Denton test governs any application to avoid it.
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.summary_line: RELIEF FROM SANCTIONS · CPR 3.9 · DENTON · THREE STAGES: SERIOUS? WHY? ALL CIRCUMSTANCES · MITCHELL · FIRM APPROACH

Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.front: What is an unless order and what happens if a party does not comply?
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.answer:
An unless order is a conditional court order stating that unless a party complies with a specified step by a specified deadline, a defined sanction will apply automatically (e.g. claim struck out, defence struck out, specific evidence excluded). Unless orders are typically made where a party has already breached a previous order.

The sanction takes effect automatically on non-compliance — no further order is required and the defaulting party need not be notified of the consequence. To avoid the sanction, the party must comply by the deadline or apply promptly for relief from sanction under CPR 3.9 (applying the Denton test).

⚠️ Link — Denton test (see FLK1-DR-057): relief from an unless order sanction is governed by the three-stage Denton framework.
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.summary_line: UNLESS ORDER · AUTOMATIC SANCTION · NON-COMPLIANCE · CPR 3.9 DENTON FOR RELIEF · NO NOTIFICATION REQUIRED

Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.front: Mitchell v News Group Newspapers Ltd [2013] EWCA Civ 1537
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.issue: Following the April 2013 amendments to CPR 3.9, what approach should courts take to applications for relief from sanctions?
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.rule: The 2013 amendments to CPR 3.9 narrowed the factors to be considered to two: (a) the need to conduct litigation efficiently and at proportionate cost; and (b) the need to enforce compliance with rules, practice directions, and court orders. The Court of Appeal held that courts should apply a firm, robust approach — taking the new CPR rules seriously and not easily granting relief for non-compliance. Even minor or trivial breaches could not be overlooked. If a default was not trivial and there was no good reason for it, relief would usually be refused.
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.application: Mitchell's solicitors filed a costs budget one day late. The court refused relief from sanction, treating Mitchell as having filed a nil budget (recoverable costs limited to court fees only). The Court of Appeal upheld this decision, endorsing the firm approach in the strongest terms — compliance with court orders and rules was not optional.
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.conclusion:
Established the post-2013 approach to CPR 3.9. However, Mitchell was rapidly criticised for producing harsh and disproportionate results in cases where the breach was trivial or unintentional.

⚠️ Mitchell was refined and in part corrected by Denton v TH White [2014] (CA), which introduced the three-stage test and clarified that courts should not apply CPR 3.9 in an opportunistic or mechanical way. Mitchell remains important as the starting point of the modern approach but must be read alongside Denton.
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.summary_line: RELIEF FROM SANCTIONS · CPR 3.9 · FIRM APPROACH · COSTS BUDGET LATE · COURTS TAKE COMPLIANCE SERIOUSLY · REFINED BY DENTON [2014]

Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.front: Denton v TH White Ltd [2014] EWCA Civ 906
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.issue: What is the correct approach to applications for relief from sanctions under CPR 3.9, following criticism that Mitchell had been applied too harshly?
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.rule:
The court applies a three-stage test:

1. Identify and assess the seriousness and significance of the breach — a trivial breach will usually attract relief without close analysis of the other stages; a serious and significant breach requires more careful consideration.
2. Consider why the default occurred — a good reason (such as accident, illness, or an error outside the party's control) may justify relief even for a serious breach; no reason or a bad reason weighs heavily against relief.
3. Evaluate all the circumstances of the case, including CPR 3.9(1)(a) (efficient litigation) and (b) (enforcement of compliance), giving appropriate weight to each.

The court should not refuse relief for trivial or technical defaults. Neither should lawyers routinely oppose relief applications where the breach has caused no prejudice.
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.application: Three conjoined appeals involving: late disclosure, service of documents one day late, and failure to serve expert evidence on time. In each case the Court of Appeal restated and refined the Mitchell approach, setting out the three stages explicitly. The CA emphasised that taking a disproportionately strict line on trivial breaches was as unacceptable as being too lenient.
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.conclusion:
The definitive authority on CPR 3.9 and relief from sanctions. Denton modifies (but does not overrule) Mitchell. The three-stage test must be applied sequentially; stage 3 only becomes significant where stages 1 and 2 do not produce a clear answer. Parties and their lawyers must not take an 'opportunistic' approach by opposing relief where there has been no real prejudice.

⚠️ The promptness of the relief application is always a relevant factor at stage 3.
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.summary_line: DENTON TEST · CPR 3.9 · THREE STAGES: SERIOUSNESS · REASON · ALL CIRCUMSTANCES · MODIFIES MITCHELL · NOT OPPORTUNISTIC

Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.front: The court orders the defendant to provide disclosure by 14 April 2026. The defendant provides nothing. On 20 April the defendant applies for relief. Disclosure is still outstanding. What is the likely outcome?
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.answer: The court applies the Denton v TH White [2014] three-stage test under CPR 3.9. Stage 1: a complete failure to provide any disclosure, with the breach ongoing six days later, is serious and significant. Stage 2: no reason is given on these facts — this weighs heavily against relief. Stage 3: all circumstances, including the need for efficient litigation and enforcement of compliance, and the late application made while the breach continues. Relief is likely to be refused or granted only on strict conditions such as an unless order. The defendant should provide the disclosure immediately as part of any application.
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.summary_line: DENTON · CPR 3.9 · SERIOUS BREACH: COMPLETE FAILURE · NO REASON GIVEN · LATE APPLICATION · RELIEF LIKELY REFUSED

Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.front: The court makes an unless order: unless the claimant serves a witness statement by 4pm on 10 April 2026, the claim shall be struck out. The claimant fails to serve by the deadline. On 12 April the claimant applies for relief and serves the witness statement. Advise.
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.answer: The claim is automatically struck out at 4pm on 10 April — no further order is required (the sanction fires automatically). The claimant must apply for relief from sanction under CPR 3.9, applying the Denton test. Stage 1: strike out is the most severe sanction — the breach is serious. Stage 2: the reason for missing the deadline must be assessed; none is given on these facts. Stage 3: all circumstances, including the two-day delay in applying and whether the defendant has suffered real prejudice. Relief is uncertain — it may be granted on terms (e.g. costs order) if there is a good reason, or refused if not. The witness statement now being available is a mitigating factor.
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.summary_line: UNLESS ORDER · AUTO STRIKE OUT AT DEADLINE · CPR 3.9 DENTON FOR RELIEF · SERIOUS BREACH · TWO-DAY DELAY · REASON CRITICAL

Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.front: Going deeper: Does the Denton test for relief from sanctions strike the right balance between procedural discipline and access to justice?
Relief from Sanctions & Unless Orders.answer:
The tension at the heart of CPR 3.9 is between two legitimate but competing values: efficiency and finality of proceedings (requiring strict compliance); and access to justice (requiring courts to decide cases on their merits even where procedural defaults occur). Mitchell v News Group Newspapers [2013] favoured efficiency heavily, producing a wave of draconian decisions that Denton v TH White [2014] sought to correct.

Lord Dyson MR in Denton expressly criticised the tactical deployment of relief from sanctions applications — exploiting minor breaches to obtain a windfall advantage — as an abuse of the CPR undermining efficiency. This is the 'opportunistic litigation' problem.

Professor Adrian Zuckerman (Oxford) argues that the problem with the pre-Mitchell regime was failure to give effect to the systemic harm of accumulated delays, not excessive leniency on individual defaults. The solution he proposes is a proportionality framework calibrated to systemic harm — which Denton partially achieves but applies imprecisely.

The tension between discipline and access to justice recurs in ECHR Art 6 jurisprudence: Zubac v Croatia [2017] (GC) reaffirms that procedural rules must not restrict access to court in a way that impairs the very essence of the right, even where the default was caused by the applicant's own lawyers.

⚠️ Not SQE-tested: SQE covers the Denton three-stage test; the access to justice jurisprudence and academic critiques are seminar material.

Civil Litigation Framework.front: What is the analytical framework for the stages of civil litigation from pre-action to enforcement?
Civil Litigation Framework.answer:
The civil litigation process follows this sequence:

1. Pre-action: comply with relevant protocol or Practice Direction; send letter of claim; consider ADR; allow reasonable time to respond.

2. Commencing proceedings: issue claim form within limitation period; serve within 4 months.

3. Statements of case: particulars of claim → defence (+/- counterclaim) → reply if required.

4. Case management: directions questionnaire → track allocation → directions order (disclosure, evidence, trial date).

5. Disclosure: standard disclosure, inspection, any specific disclosure applications.

6. Evidence: exchange witness statements; serve expert reports; SJE or party experts.

7. Pre-trial: costs budgeting (multi-track); pre-trial checklist; trial bundle.

8. Trial: hearing → judgment.

9. Costs: summary or detailed assessment.

10. Enforcement if defendant does not pay voluntarily.

11. Appeals: permission → appeal hearing on review.

⚠️ SQE questions frequently test whether a step has been taken at the right stage or out of sequence. Always locate the scenario within this framework before analysing the specific rule.
Civil Litigation Framework.summary_line: CIVIL LITIGATION STAGES · PRE-ACTION · ISSUE · STATEMENTS OF CASE · CASE MANAGEMENT · DISCLOSURE · EVIDENCE · TRIAL · COSTS · ENFORCEMENT · APPEAL

Costs Budgeting.front: In a multi-track case with a CCMC on 15 June 2026, the claimant files a Precedent H on 25 May (21 days before) and the defendant files on 26 May (20 days before). What are the consequences for the defendant?
Costs Budgeting.answer:
The claimant filed exactly 21 days before the CCMC — within the CPR 3.13 deadline. The defendant filed 20 days before — one day late. Under CPR 3.14, a party that fails to file a budget by the deadline is treated as having filed a budget comprising only court fees. This is automatic — the defendant's recoverable costs (other than court fees) are nil unless it obtains relief from sanction under CPR 3.9 (Denton). The defendant should apply for relief immediately, ideally before the CCMC.

⚠️ The CPR 3.14 sanction is severe and automatic — one day late is sufficient to trigger it.
Costs Budgeting.summary_line: COSTS BUDGETING · PRECEDENT H · CLAIMANT IN TIME · DEFENDANT LATE BY ONE DAY · CPR 3.14 SANCTION: COURT FEES ONLY · DENTON RELIEF NEEDED

Costs Budgeting.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

In a multi-track case, the claimant's solicitors file their Precedent H 20 days before the CCMC — one day after the CPR 3.13 deadline of 21 days. The claimant's solicitor tells the client not to worry because the court will take a proportionate approach and any sanction will be modest.
Costs Budgeting.issue: The Trap: Students who know the Denton test know courts take a proportionate approach to breaches. They assume that a one-day delay will attract only a modest penalty — a costs order or a small reduction. The breach is trivial, so the sanction must be proportionate to the breach.
Costs Budgeting.rule: The Reality: Under CPR 3.14, the sanction for failing to file a costs budget by the deadline is automatic and categorical: the party is treated as having filed a budget comprising only court fees. This is not a discretionary sanction calibrated to the gravity of the breach — it is the prescribed consequence. One day late and the claimant's entire solicitors' costs recovery is reduced to court fees only. To escape, the claimant must apply for relief from sanction under CPR 3.9 and satisfy the Denton test.
Costs Budgeting.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is knowledge of the Denton test, which governs relief from sanctions generally. Students apply it here because there is a missed deadline. But CPR 3.14 creates its own automatic sanction for costs budgets with no discretion to apply Denton — the rule is categorical and the Denton framework does not apply.
Costs Budgeting.conclusion: The Key Rule: CPR 3.14 imposes an automatic and categorical sanction — nil recoverable costs (court fees only) — for late filing of a costs budget, regardless of the degree of lateness. The court does not scale the sanction to the length of the delay. The only route to escape is a CPR 3.9 Denton relief application. 'Proportionate' discretion applies to whether Denton relief should be granted — not to the sanction itself.
Costs Budgeting.summary_line: COSTS BUDGETING · CPR 3.14 · AUTOMATIC SANCTION: COURT FEES ONLY · ONE DAY LATE = FULL SANCTION · NOT SCALED TO DELAY · DENTON RELIEF ONLY ROUTE

Standard Disclosure.front: What does standard disclosure require, and how does it differ from the pre-CPR 'train of inquiry' test?
Standard Disclosure.answer:
Standard disclosure requires each party to disclose documents that: (a) they rely on; (b) adversely affect their own case; (c) adversely affect another party's case; or (d) support another party's case (CPR 31.6). It does not extend to documents that merely might lead to a train of inquiry that could produce relevant material — that was the broader pre-CPR Peruvian Guano test.

Documents must be listed in a disclosure list. The duty applies to documents within a party's control — i.e. in their possession, or to which they have a right to possession or inspection.

⚠️ Standard disclosure is significantly narrower than the old train of inquiry test. A common exam error is to overstate the scope of disclosure required.
Standard Disclosure.summary_line: STANDARD DISCLOSURE · CPR 31.6 · FOUR CATEGORIES · NOT TRAIN OF INQUIRY · DOCUMENTS IN CONTROL · PERUVIAN GUANO

Standard Disclosure.front: What is the scope of the duty to search for disclosable documents?
Standard Disclosure.answer:
A party must conduct a reasonable search for disclosable documents (CPR 31.7). The reasonableness of the search depends on:

1. the number of documents involved
2. the nature and complexity of the proceedings
3. the ease and expense of retrieval; and
4. the significance of any document likely to be located.

A party may limit their search on proportionality grounds, but must state the limitations in their disclosure statement (and explain why further search is disproportionate). Electronic documents require particular attention — the court may give specific e-disclosure directions on keyword searches, date ranges, and custodians.
Standard Disclosure.summary_line: DUTY TO SEARCH · CPR 31.7 · REASONABLE SEARCH · PROPORTIONALITY · DISCLOSURE STATEMENT · E-DISCLOSURE

Standard Disclosure.front: In a contract dispute, the defendant discloses the contract and correspondence. The claimant demands disclosure of internal emails between the defendant's staff discussing the claimant's performance, which were not disclosed. Is the defendant obliged to disclose them?
Standard Disclosure.answer: Standard disclosure under CPR 31.6 requires disclosure of documents that: (a) the defendant relies on; (b) adversely affect the defendant's own case; (c) adversely affect the claimant's case; or (d) support the claimant's case. If the internal emails fall into any of these categories — for example, because they show internal doubts about the defendant's own position (adversely affecting its case) or support the claimant's case — they must be disclosed. The defendant cannot withhold documents in categories (b)–(d) simply because they are internal. Whether the emails are disclosable depends on their content, not on whether the defendant chooses to rely on them.
Standard Disclosure.summary_line: STANDARD DISCLOSURE · CPR 31.6 · FOUR CATEGORIES · INTERNAL EMAILS · ADVERSE TO OWN CASE OR SUPPORTS CLAIMANT · CANNOT WITHHOLD

Privilege.front: What are the two main categories of legal professional privilege and how do they differ?
Privilege.answer:
Legal professional privilege (LPP) has two categories:

1. Legal advice privilege: protects confidential communications between a lawyer and client made for the purpose of giving or receiving legal advice — even if litigation is not contemplated.

2. Litigation privilege: protects confidential communications between a lawyer, client, or third parties made for the dominant purpose of litigation that is reasonably anticipated or already on foot.

LPP is absolute — it cannot be overridden by considerations of relevance, proportionality, or public interest. It belongs to the client (not the lawyer) and only the client can waive it.

⚠️ The dominant purpose test is key to litigation privilege. A document created for both litigation and commercial purposes may not qualify — the litigation purpose must dominate.

⚠️ Link — Without prejudice privilege (see FLK1-DR-027): LPP and WP privilege are distinct and should not be conflated.
Privilege.summary_line: LPP · LEGAL ADVICE PRIVILEGE · LITIGATION PRIVILEGE · DOMINANT PURPOSE · ABSOLUTE · CLIENT WAIVES · CPR 31.19

Privilege.front: What is without prejudice privilege, when does it apply, and what are its limits?
Privilege.answer:
Without prejudice (WP) privilege protects communications made in a genuine attempt to settle a dispute from being used as evidence in proceedings. It applies where:

1. there is an existing dispute; and
2. the communication is a genuine attempt to settle it.

Marking a document 'without prejudice' is not conclusive — the court looks at the substance.

The privilege is joint — both parties must agree to waive it.

**Limits:** WP privilege does not apply where: (a) the communication is used to establish that a settlement was reached (a concluded agreement); (b) the communication constitutes a clear misrepresentation, fraud, or unambiguous impropriety; or (c) it is used to show the existence of the negotiations (not their content).

⚠️ Link — LPP (see FLK1-DR-026): WP protects settlement negotiations; LPP protects lawyer-client communications. They are separate doctrines.

⚠️ Link — Part 36 (see FLK1-DR-039): Part 36 offers are made 'without prejudice save as to costs' — a statutory modification of WP permitting the offer to be disclosed to the judge on costs after judgment.
Privilege.summary_line: WITHOUT PREJUDICE · SETTLEMENT NEGOTIATIONS · GENUINE ATTEMPT · JOINT PRIVILEGE · LIMITS: CONCLUDED SETTLEMENT · FRAUD

Privilege.front: Waugh v British Railways Board [1980] AC 521
Privilege.issue: Whether an internal accident report prepared partly for submission to management and partly in anticipation of litigation attracted litigation privilege.
Privilege.rule: For a document to attract litigation privilege, litigation must be the dominant purpose for which it was brought into existence. Where a document is prepared for two purposes — internal management/safety and potential litigation — it will only be privileged if the litigation purpose clearly dominates the other purposes. A purpose that is merely one of several significant purposes, even an important one, will not suffice.
Privilege.application: British Railways' internal report following a fatal accident was prepared for two purposes: submission to management for safety improvements, and potential use in personal injury litigation. The House of Lords held that the dominant purpose was management/safety, not litigation — therefore the report was not privileged and had to be disclosed.
Privilege.conclusion:
The definitive authority on the dominant purpose test for litigation privilege. In practice, organisations now frequently create two separate documents after incidents: one for internal safety/management purposes (not privileged), and a separate legally privileged report prepared by lawyers exclusively for litigation. The two must be genuinely separate — a dual-purpose document will fail the Waugh test.
