⚠️ Link to legal advice privilege (FLK1-DR-026): the dominant purpose test applies to litigation privilege only — legal advice privilege (covering lawyer–client advice) does not require litigation to be in prospect.
Privilege.summary_line: LITIGATION PRIVILEGE · DOMINANT PURPOSE TEST · DUAL-PURPOSE DOCUMENT · ACCIDENT REPORTS · NOT PRIVILEGED IF SAFETY DOMINANT

Privilege.front: Rush & Tompkins Ltd v Greater London Council [1989] AC 1280
Privilege.issue: Whether without prejudice communications between two parties could be disclosed in subsequent proceedings brought by a third party.
Privilege.rule: Without prejudice privilege protects settlement negotiations not only in the proceedings in which they were generated, but also in subsequent proceedings involving the same parties or third parties. The privilege cannot be unilaterally waived by one party — it is a joint privilege. Even where one of the original parties settles and leaves the litigation, the without prejudice communications remain protected from disclosure in the continuing proceedings.
Privilege.application: Rush & Tompkins (main contractor) settled its dispute with the GLC. A sub-contractor then sought disclosure of the without prejudice negotiations between Rush & Tompkins and the GLC, to assist with its own claim. The House of Lords held the without prejudice communications remained privileged and could not be disclosed to the sub-contractor.
Privilege.conclusion:
Established the broad reach of without prejudice privilege — it is not limited to the specific proceedings it arose in and survives the settlement of those proceedings. This is particularly significant in construction and multi-party litigation where claimants often try to use prior settlements as evidence of the value of their own claims.

⚠️ The privilege has exceptions: a concluded agreement; unambiguous impropriety; and a few others (see Unilever plc v Procter & Gamble [2000]).
Privilege.summary_line: WITHOUT PREJUDICE PRIVILEGE · THIRD PARTY CANNOT OBTAIN · JOINT PRIVILEGE · SURVIVES SETTLEMENT · CONSTRUCTION LITIGATION

Privilege.front: Unilever plc v Procter & Gamble Co [2000] 1 WLR 2436
Privilege.issue: What are the recognised exceptions to without prejudice privilege, and in particular what constitutes 'unambiguous impropriety' sufficient to bring communications out of protection?
Privilege.rule:
Without prejudice privilege has a number of recognised exceptions, including where:

1. the issue is whether negotiations produced a concluded agreement
2. the communications are used to explain or give context to a concluded agreement
3. there is a clear misrepresentation, fraud, or 'unambiguous impropriety'
4. the communications are relied on to show that a party has waived a right
5. the communications are used to explain delay.

The unambiguous impropriety exception requires conduct that is clearly improper — not merely aggressive, ambitious, or commercially robust negotiating tactics.
Privilege.application: In patent infringement negotiations, Procter & Gamble's representatives made statements that Unilever contended were threatening and improper. The Court of Appeal held that although the statements were robust and aggressive, they did not cross the threshold of unambiguous impropriety. The exception is narrow and requires conduct that is an abuse of the WP process itself.
Privilege.conclusion: The leading authority cataloguing the exceptions to without prejudice privilege and defining the limits of the 'unambiguous impropriety' exception. The exception is deliberately narrow — courts are reluctant to undermine the without prejudice principle, which depends on parties being able to negotiate freely without fear of their words being used against them. Strong language or threats to pursue aggressive litigation strategies do not amount to impropriety.
Privilege.summary_line: WITHOUT PREJUDICE · EXCEPTIONS · UNAMBIGUOUS IMPROPRIETY · NARROW EXCEPTION · CONCLUDED AGREEMENT · FRAUD · AGGRESSIVE TACTICS INSUFFICIENT

Privilege.front: After a factory accident, the company's solicitor prepares an accident report requested by both the health and safety department and the legal team for potential litigation. The claimant demands disclosure. Can the company claim litigation privilege?
Privilege.answer:
This follows the Waugh v British Railways Board [1980] dominant purpose test. For litigation privilege, the dominant purpose for which the document was created must be preparation for litigation. Here the report was commissioned for two purposes: regulatory/H&S compliance and potential litigation. Unless the litigation purpose clearly dominates, privilege will not apply. Analogous to Waugh — where the dominant purpose was management/safety — the company is likely to struggle to establish that litigation dominated. The report will probably not attract litigation privilege and must be disclosed.

⚠️ To protect future reports, companies should create two separate documents: one for safety purposes (not privileged) and one exclusively prepared by lawyers for litigation.
Privilege.summary_line: LITIGATION PRIVILEGE · DOMINANT PURPOSE TEST · WAUGH · DUAL PURPOSE: H&S AND LITIGATION · LITIGATION NOT DOMINANT · NOT PRIVILEGED

Privilege.front: During settlement negotiations, Harriet's solicitor writes a letter headed 'Without Prejudice' offering to accept 60% of the claimed sum. The defendant does not accept. At trial, the defendant's barrister seeks to use the letter to argue Harriet has impliedly conceded weakness in her case. Can it be used?
Privilege.answer: No. The letter is protected by without prejudice privilege. WP privilege protects genuine attempts to settle a dispute from being used as evidence. The letter is headed without prejudice and represents a genuine settlement offer. None of the exceptions apply — there is no concluded agreement, no unambiguous impropriety, and a settlement offer at a discount is normal commercial negotiation. Using the letter to imply Harriet recognised weakness in her case is precisely what WP privilege is designed to prevent. The defendant's barrister's attempt must be rejected.
Privilege.summary_line: WITHOUT PREJUDICE · GENUINE SETTLEMENT ATTEMPT · CANNOT USE AS EVIDENCE · CONCESSION ARGUMENT FAILS · NONE OF THE EXCEPTIONS APPLY

Privilege.front: Going deeper: What is the proper scope of legal advice privilege in large organisations — and does Three Rivers DC v Bank of England (No 6) [2004] produce incoherent results?
Privilege.answer:
Three Rivers DC v Bank of England (No 6) [2004] held that legal advice privilege (LAP) attaches only to communications between the lawyer and the specific individuals who constitute the 'client' for the purposes of the retainer. In a large organisation, internal reports from employees who were not themselves part of the designated client team were not covered by LAP — only communications from those authorised to seek advice qualified.

This produces paradoxical results criticised extensively by Hollander QC, Thanki QC, and academic commentators including Professor Colin Tapper. A large corporation may find that the very communications necessary to bring a legal matter to its solicitors are unprotected — the lawyers' advice is privileged, but the background materials assembling that advice are not.

The Law Commission considered LPP reform in its 2011 report on Fiduciary Duties but did not directly address the Three Rivers problem. Three Rivers has been applied narrowly by subsequent courts (RBS Rights Issue Litigation [2016]). Whether the Supreme Court will revisit the decision — and whether English law should adopt a broader approach similar to many civil law systems — remains live.

⚠️ Not SQE-tested: the SQE tests LAP at the level of its basic operation; Three Rivers analysis is seminar-level depth.

Privilege.front: Going deeper: Is the without prejudice rule too broad — and does it protect improper conduct at the expense of justice?
Privilege.answer:
The without prejudice rule rests on public policy (encouraging settlement) and implied contract (parties negotiate on the implied term that statements will not be used). Hoffmann LJ in Muller v Linsley & Mortimer [1996] identified these as separate foundations, producing uncertainty in edge cases.

The rule has been criticised — notably by Lightman J in Ferster v Farhan [2016] — for protecting aggressive and commercially oppressive negotiating conduct under the 'unambiguous impropriety' exception. Walker LJ in Unilever v Procter & Gamble [2000] set this threshold high to prevent the exception from swallowing the rule, but Professor Adrian Zuckerman (Civil Procedure, 5th ed) argues the threshold is too high and allows parties to abuse the process.

A related tension arises in multi-party litigation: Rush & Tompkins [1989] established that WP communications cannot be disclosed to third parties even after settlement. This can produce information asymmetry — a settling defendant may have obtained information about the claimant's position that other defendants cannot access, distorting subsequent negotiations.

Berry Trade Ltd v Moussavi [2003] confirmed the WP rule can apply even when communications are not expressly labelled WP, creating uncertainty about which commercial communications are protected.

⚠️ Not SQE-tested: SQE covers the core WP rule; academic critiques are seminar material.

Privilege.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A solicitor sends a letter headed 'Without Prejudice' to the defendant, offering to settle the claim. The defendant's solicitor asks for the letter to be disclosed to the defendant's insurer. The claimant's solicitor refuses, saying the letter is protected by legal professional privilege and cannot be disclosed under any circumstances.
Privilege.issue: The Trap: A letter marked 'without prejudice' feels like it is protected from disclosure. Students who know that LPP is absolute may assume this absolute protection also applies to WP communications — particularly when the claimant's solicitor invokes the combination of 'without prejudice' and absolute privilege.
Privilege.rule: The Reality: LPP and WP privilege are entirely separate doctrines. LPP protects confidential lawyer-client communications made for the purpose of legal advice or litigation. WP privilege protects genuine settlement negotiations between the parties. A letter sent to the opposing party is not a lawyer-client communication — it cannot attract LPP. It may attract WP privilege, but WP privilege is not absolute: it has recognised exceptions and belongs jointly to both parties.
Privilege.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is the 'without prejudice' label on the letter. Students who know WP privilege assume the label is decisive. But WP and LPP are entirely separate doctrines with different scope. The letter here is a solicitor's advice to the client — LPP attaches to that relationship. The WP label is irrelevant to whether LPP applies.
Privilege.conclusion: The Key Rule: Without prejudice privilege and LPP are distinct doctrines. LPP protects confidential lawyer-client communications and is absolute. WP privilege protects genuine settlement negotiations between the parties and is not absolute — it has exceptions and is a joint privilege. A letter sent to opposing solicitors cannot be protected by LPP.
Privilege.summary_line: WP PRIVILEGE ≠ LPP · SEPARATE DOCTRINES · LPP IS ABSOLUTE · WP IS NOT ABSOLUTE · WP HAS EXCEPTIONS · JOINT PRIVILEGE · LETTER TO OPPONENT CANNOT BE LPP

Specific Disclosure.front: When may a party apply for specific disclosure and what can the court order?
Specific Disclosure.answer:
A party may apply for specific disclosure (CPR 31.12) where they believe the other party has failed to carry out standard disclosure adequately. The court may order:

1. disclosure of specific documents or classes of documents
2. a further search for documents on specified terms; or
3. inspection of documents already disclosed but withheld on claimed grounds.

The applicant must establish that the documents exist (or are likely to) and fall within the categories for standard disclosure. The court applies proportionality — specific disclosure will not be ordered if the cost and burden are disproportionate to its likely benefit.
Specific Disclosure.summary_line: SPECIFIC DISCLOSURE · CPR 31.12 · FAILED STANDARD DISCLOSURE · PROPORTIONALITY

Specific Disclosure.front: In personal injury litigation, the defendant insurer provided standard disclosure but the claimant believes the defendant holds undisclosed surveillance footage. How should the claimant proceed?
Specific Disclosure.answer:
The claimant should apply for specific disclosure under CPR 31.12. To succeed, the claimant must establish:

1. the surveillance footage is likely to exist; and
2. it falls within the four categories of standard disclosure — it will adversely affect the defendant's case if it shows injuries are genuine, or adversely affect the claimant's case if it shows exaggeration (in which case the defendant should already have disclosed it).

The court will order specific disclosure if the footage is likely to exist and likely to be disclosable. If the non-disclosure appears intentional, an adverse inference may also be drawn at trial.
Specific Disclosure.summary_line: SPECIFIC DISCLOSURE · CPR 31.12 · SURVEILLANCE FOOTAGE · LIKELY TO EXIST · CPR 31.6 CATEGORIES · ADVERSE INFERENCE IF INTENTIONAL

Pre-Action & Non-Party Disclosure.front: What are the requirements for obtaining pre-action disclosure and disclosure against non-parties?
Pre-Action & Non-Party Disclosure.answer:
Pre-action disclosure (CPR 31.16): the court may order a respondent to disclose documents before proceedings are issued where:

1. both the applicant and respondent are likely to be parties to subsequent proceedings
2. if proceedings had been started, the respondent's duty of standard disclosure would extend to the documents; and
3. pre-action disclosure is desirable to fairly dispose of the anticipated proceedings, to assist in resolving the dispute without proceedings, or to save costs.

Non-party disclosure (CPR 31.17): once proceedings are issued, the court may order a non-party to disclose documents relevant to an issue in the proceedings where:
1. the documents are likely to support the applicant's case or adversely affect another party's case; and
2. disclosure is necessary to dispose fairly of the proceedings or to save costs.

Both applications require the court's permission. Costs are usually awarded in favour of the non-party or respondent.

⚠️ CPR 31.16 is pre-action (both likely parties); CPR 31.17 is against a non-party during live proceedings. This distinction is frequently tested.
Pre-Action & Non-Party Disclosure.summary_line: PRE-ACTION DISCLOSURE · CPR 31.16 · LIKELY PARTIES · NON-PARTY DISCLOSURE · CPR 31.17 · DESIRABLE/NECESSARY · COSTS TO RESPONDENT

Pre-Action & Non-Party Disclosure.front: Julia has been injured in a road accident but is uncertain whether the vehicle was owned by the driver or a hire company. She has not yet issued proceedings. She wants disclosure from the hire company to identify the correct defendant. Advise.
Pre-Action & Non-Party Disclosure.answer:
Julia should apply for pre-action disclosure under CPR 31.16. The conditions are:

1. both Julia and the hire company are likely to be parties to subsequent proceedings
2. if proceedings were issued, standard disclosure would extend to the ownership documents; and
3. pre-action disclosure is desirable to fairly dispose of the anticipated proceedings or save costs.

All three conditions are arguably satisfied — identifying the correct defendant before issue saves costs and avoids naming the wrong party. The court retains a discretion; costs are usually awarded to the respondent.

⚠️ CPR 31.16 is pre-issue against a likely party; CPR 31.17 applies once proceedings are on foot against a non-party.
Pre-Action & Non-Party Disclosure.summary_line: PRE-ACTION DISCLOSURE · CPR 31.16 · BOTH LIKELY PARTIES · DESIRABLE · IDENTIFY CORRECT DEFENDANT · COSTS TO RESPONDENT · DISTINGUISH CPR 31.17

Witness Evidence.front: What are the requirements for a witness statement and what is its function at trial?
Witness Evidence.answer:
A witness statement must (PD 32):

1. be in the witness's own words
2. contain only facts within the witness's direct knowledge
3. be signed with a statement of truth
4. comply with the prescribed format (full name, address, occupation, exhibit references).

A witness statement stands as the witness's examination-in-chief at trial — the witness will not be asked to repeat its contents orally. The witness is then available for cross-examination.

A party who intends not to call a witness whose statement has been served must notify the other parties. Those parties may then put the statement in evidence themselves if they wish.
Witness Evidence.summary_line: WITNESS STATEMENT · OWN WORDS · DIRECT KNOWLEDGE · STATEMENT OF TRUTH · STANDS AS EXAMINATION-IN-CHIEF · PD 32

Witness Evidence.front: A claimant's solicitor proposes to adduce at trial a witness statement from a colleague who has since emigrated and is not being called. The defendant objects. Can the statement be used?
Witness Evidence.answer: Yes, subject to compliance with the hearsay notice procedure under the Civil Evidence Act 1995. The witness statement constitutes hearsay evidence. Under s1 CEA 1995, hearsay is admissible in civil proceedings. The claimant must give notice under s2 CEA 1995. If notice is given (or given promptly), the defendant's admissibility objection fails. However, the court will consider weight under s4 CEA 1995 — factors include whether the maker could have given direct evidence, contemporaneity, and that the evidence cannot be tested in cross-examination. The defendant may call their own evidence to contradict it.
Witness Evidence.summary_line: HEARSAY · ADMISSIBLE CIVIL · CEA 1995 · NOTICE REQUIRED s2 · WEIGHT UNDER s4 · CANNOT CROSS-EXAMINE · DEFENDANT MAY CONTRADICT

Witness Evidence.front: At trial, the claimant's barrister wants to re-examine their witness and proposes to ask about a new topic not raised in cross-examination. Is this permissible?
Witness Evidence.answer: No. Re-examination is strictly limited to matters arising from cross-examination. Its purpose is to allow the witness to explain or clarify matters raised or damaged in cross-examination — it is not an opportunity to introduce new topics. If the barrister attempts to ask about a new topic, the judge will disallow the question. If genuinely new evidence has emerged that was not in the witness statement and not covered in cross-examination, the claimant would need to apply to adduce further evidence under CPR 32, which requires permission and is only granted in exceptional circumstances.
Witness Evidence.summary_line: RE-EXAMINATION · LIMITED TO MATTERS ARISING FROM CROSS · NEW TOPICS NOT PERMITTED · JUDGE WILL DISALLOW · APPLICATION FOR FURTHER EVIDENCE IF NEEDED

Expert Evidence.front: What is the general rule on expert evidence in civil proceedings and what duty does an expert owe?
Expert Evidence.answer:
Expert evidence requires permission from the court (CPR 35.4). The court restricts expert evidence to that which is reasonably required to resolve the proceedings. Where possible, a single joint expert (SJE) is used rather than separate party experts.

An expert's overriding duty is to the court, not to the party instructing them (CPR 35.3). This duty overrides any obligation to the party paying them. Every expert report must contain a statement confirming the expert understands their duty and has complied with it.

An expert who acts as an advocate risks having their evidence excluded and faces an adverse costs order.
Expert Evidence.summary_line: EXPERT EVIDENCE · COURT PERMISSION · DUTY TO COURT CPR 35.3 · SJE PREFERRED · OVERRIDES INSTRUCTING PARTY · CPR 35.4

Expert Evidence.front: When will the court order a single joint expert rather than allow each party to instruct their own?
Expert Evidence.answer:
The court will generally order a single joint expert (SJE) where:

1. the issue is not central to the case
2. separate experts would be disproportionate to the value or complexity; or
3. the factual framework is broadly agreed and the expert is needed only to give a technical opinion within it.

In fast track cases, a SJE is the strong default position. In multi-track cases, the court has broader discretion but will still evaluate proportionality carefully.

Where separate party experts are permitted, the court usually directs a without prejudice meeting between them to narrow issues, followed by a joint statement identifying areas of agreement and disagreement — this is filed and may not be amended without the court's permission.
Expert Evidence.summary_line: SINGLE JOINT EXPERT · FAST TRACK DEFAULT · MULTI-TRACK DISCRETION · JOINT STATEMENT · WITHOUT PREJUDICE MEETING

Expert Evidence.front: What must an expert's report contain?
Expert Evidence.answer:
An expert's report must (CPR 35.10 and PD 35):

1. give the expert's opinion and the reasoning behind it
2. state the substance of all material instructions (written and oral)
3. include a statement that the expert understands their overriding duty to the court and has complied with it
4. be verified by a statement of truth
5. set out any range of opinion on the issue and the reasons for the expert's position within that range
6. summarise the expert's conclusions; and
7. indicate if any question is outside the expert's expertise.

Instructions to an expert are not privileged in the sense that the court may order disclosure if there are reasonable grounds to believe the summary of instructions is inaccurate or incomplete.
Expert Evidence.summary_line: EXPERT REPORT · CPR 35.10 · REASONING · INSTRUCTIONS · DUTY TO COURT STATEMENT · RANGE OF OPINION · STATEMENT OF TRUTH

Expert Evidence.front: In a fast track personal injury claim, the claimant asks for permission to instruct their own medical expert separately from any jointly instructed expert. Is this likely to be permitted?
Expert Evidence.answer: On the fast track the strong default is a single joint expert (SJE). The court will only permit separate party experts in fast track cases in exceptional circumstances — typically where the issues are too complex or contested for a SJE to be adequate. In a personal injury claim on the fast track, a SJE for medical evidence is the norm. Permission for a separate expert is unlikely. If separate experts are permitted (unusual), the court will direct a without prejudice discussion and joint statement. The fast track is specifically structured around the SJE principle to reduce cost and adversarial expert evidence.
Expert Evidence.summary_line: EXPERT EVIDENCE · FAST TRACK · SJE STRONG DEFAULT · SEPARATE EXPERT UNLIKELY · EXCEPTIONAL CIRCUMSTANCES · JOINT STATEMENT

Expert Evidence.front: An expert instructed by the defendant files a report strongly favouring the defendant. In cross-examination the expert admits they discussed limiting adverse findings with the defendant's solicitors before finalising the report. What are the consequences?
Expert Evidence.answer:
An expert's overriding duty is to the court, not the instructing party (CPR 35.3). Discussing and limiting adverse findings with the instructing solicitor is a serious breach of this duty, undermining the expert's independence. Consequences may include:

1. the court giving significantly reduced weight to or disregarding the expert's evidence entirely
2. an adverse costs order against the defendant for relying on a partisan expert
3. possible referral to the expert's professional body.

The expert's statement confirming compliance with their duty to the court (required in all expert reports) would also have been false. The defendant's case is seriously damaged.
Expert Evidence.summary_line: EXPERT DUTY TO COURT · CPR 35.3 · OVERRIDING DUTY · LIMITING ADVERSE FINDINGS = BREACH · EVIDENCE DISREGARDED · ADVERSE COSTS · PROFESSIONAL REFERRAL

Expert Evidence.front: Two permitted party experts meet without prejudice but cannot agree on the value of a disputed property, with a £100,000 difference in valuations. The parties ask whether they need a further expert. Advise.
Expert Evidence.answer: Where two party experts cannot agree, the correct procedure is to file a joint statement setting out: (a) matters they agree on; (b) matters they disagree on; (c) reasons for each disagreement. The joint statement cannot be amended without the court's permission. It must be filed before trial so the court can see which issues remain genuinely disputed. The court resolves the disagreement at trial by hearing from both experts. The parties do not need a further (third) expert — the procedure is designed to narrow, not eliminate, disagreement. Commissioning a further expert without court permission would itself require an application under CPR 35.4.
Expert Evidence.summary_line: PARTY EXPERTS · JOINT STATEMENT · AGREE/DISAGREE/REASONS · NO FURTHER EXPERT NEEDED · COURT RESOLVES AT TRIAL · CANNOT AMEND WITHOUT PERMISSION

Expert Evidence.front: Going deeper: What is the Ikarian Reefer principle of expert independence, and does the single joint expert regime adequately address the problem of partisan experts?
Expert Evidence.answer:
In The Ikarian Reefer [1993], Cresswell J set out the principles governing expert witnesses in civil litigation:

1. independent assistance to the court by objective, unbiased opinion
2. never acting as advocate
3. clearly identifying what is within and outside the expert's expertise
4. distinguishing fact from opinion; and
5. communicating changes of view promptly.

The SJE regime in CPR 35 was designed in part to address partisan experts — the well-documented tendency of party-instructed experts to favour the party paying them (Genn, 'Expert Evidence', 1998; Law Commission Consultation Paper No 154, 1996).

However, the SJE model has attracted criticism:
1. in commercially significant cases a single expert may be inadequate
2. the SJE is selected and paid by the parties, creating potential for subtle influence
3. the joint statement procedure has been criticised for producing 'meaningless' statements in which experts agree to disagree on everything without narrowing issues.

The abolition of expert immunity in Jones v Kaney [2011] (SC) — reversing Stanton v Callaghan [2000] — was intended to promote independence through accountability. This was controversial — the concern being that experts would become more cautious and defensive as a result.

⚠️ Not SQE-tested: SQE covers expert duties, SJE, and joint statements at the practical level; Ikarian Reefer and reform debates are beyond the syllabus.

Hearsay.front: Is hearsay evidence admissible in civil proceedings and what procedural requirements apply?
Hearsay.answer:
Yes. Hearsay evidence is admissible in civil proceedings under the Civil Evidence Act 1995 (s1 CEA 1995). There is no rule against hearsay in civil cases — unlike in criminal proceedings.

A party who intends to rely on hearsay evidence must give notice to the other parties (s2 CEA 1995), unless the court dispenses with this requirement or the evidence is in a witness statement and the maker is being called to give oral evidence.

The weight given to hearsay evidence is a matter for the court, which considers factors including: whether the maker could have given direct evidence; whether the evidence was made contemporaneously; and whether it has been challenged (s4 CEA 1995).

⚠️ Hearsay is admissible in civil proceedings (CEA 1995) but generally inadmissible in criminal proceedings unless a statutory exception applies (CJA 2003). This contrast is a frequent exam distractor.
Hearsay.summary_line: HEARSAY · ADMISSIBLE CIVIL · CEA 1995 · NOTICE REQUIREMENT s2 · WEIGHT FOR COURT s4 · CONTRAST CRIMINAL CJA 2003

Interim Injunctions.front: What is the American Cyanamid test for the grant of an interim injunction?
Interim Injunctions.answer:
Under American Cyanamid Co v Ethicon Ltd [1975] AC 396, the court applies a sequential three-stage test:

1. Is there a serious question to be tried? The claimant does not need a prima facie case — only that the claim is not frivolous or vexatious.

2. Are damages an adequate remedy for the claimant? If yes, an injunction should normally be refused.

3. If damages are not adequate for the claimant, can the defendant be adequately compensated in damages by the claimant's cross-undertaking if the injunction is wrongly granted? If yes, the injunction will usually be granted. If not, the balance of convenience is considered.

⚠️

**Exception:** where the grant would restrain publication, a higher threshold applies under s12
3. HRA 1998 — the court must be satisfied the applicant is likely to succeed at trial.

⚠️ Link — Undertaking in damages (see FLK1-DR-062): every interim injunction requires a cross-undertaking in damages from the applicant.
Interim Injunctions.summary_line: INTERIM INJUNCTION · AMERICAN CYANAMID [1975] · SERIOUS QUESTION · DAMAGES ADEQUATE · BALANCE OF CONVENIENCE · s12 HRA EXCEPTION

Interim Injunctions.front: What is a cross-undertaking in damages and when is it required?
Interim Injunctions.answer:
When the court grants an interim injunction (including freezing injunctions and search orders), the applicant must give a cross-undertaking in damages — a promise to compensate the respondent for any loss caused by the injunction if it is later found to have been wrongly granted.

The undertaking is a condition of granting the injunction, not a discretionary addition. If the applicant ultimately loses and the injunction is discharged, the respondent may apply for an inquiry into damages against the undertaking.

The court may require the undertaking to be fortified — supported by a payment into court, a bank guarantee, or a bond — where the applicant's means are limited or the potential loss to the respondent is high. Publicly funded claimants give qualified undertakings; the Crown gives an undertaking as a matter of practice.
Interim Injunctions.summary_line: CROSS-UNDERTAKING IN DAMAGES · CONDITION OF INJUNCTION · INQUIRY INTO DAMAGES · FORTIFICATION · PUBLICLY FUNDED CLAIMANTS

Interim Injunctions.front: American Cyanamid Co v Ethicon Ltd [1975] AC 396
Interim Injunctions.issue: What is the correct test for granting an interim injunction, and in particular whether an applicant must establish a prima facie case?
Interim Injunctions.rule: The court applies a three-stage sequential test. First: is there a serious question to be tried (i.e. is the claim not frivolous or vexatious)? The claimant does not need to show a prima facie case. Second: if the claim clears that threshold, are damages an adequate remedy for the claimant? If yes, an injunction should normally be refused. Third: if damages are not adequate for the claimant, would the defendant be adequately compensated by the claimant's cross-undertaking in damages if the injunction is wrongly granted? If yes, the balance tips toward granting the injunction. Only if both parties face an inadequate damages remedy does the court proceed to balance convenience generally.
Interim Injunctions.application: A patent dispute over dissolvable surgical sutures. The Court of Appeal had required the applicant to show a prima facie case. The House of Lords reversed this, holding that the correct test required only a serious question to be tried. On the facts, the balance of convenience favoured granting the injunction pending trial.
Interim Injunctions.conclusion:
The foundational authority on interim injunctions, applied in virtually every application. The shift from 'prima facie case' to 'serious question to be tried' significantly lowered the threshold, but the balance of convenience stage gives courts wide discretion.

⚠️

**Exception:** where the grant would restrain publication, s12(3) HRA 1998 requires the court to be satisfied the applicant is likely to succeed at trial — a higher threshold than American Cyanamid.
Interim Injunctions.summary_line: INTERIM INJUNCTION · SERIOUS QUESTION TO BE TRIED · NOT PRIMA FACIE · DAMAGES ADEQUATE? · BALANCE OF CONVENIENCE · CROSS-UNDERTAKING

Interim Injunctions.front: Lucy discovers her former employee has set up a competing business apparently using her confidential client database. She wants to prevent the former employee contacting those clients until trial. Advise on the basis for an interim injunction.
Interim Injunctions.answer: Lucy should apply for an interim injunction under the American Cyanamid test. Stage 1: Is there a serious question to be tried? Yes — there is a plainly arguable claim for misuse of confidential information. Stage 2: Are damages an adequate remedy? Probably not — ongoing interference with client relationships may cause unquantifiable loss of goodwill. Stage 3: Would the defendant be adequately compensated by the cross-undertaking? Lucy must give a cross-undertaking in damages as a condition of any order. Lucy should also ensure the application is made promptly — delay undermines the urgency argument and may affect the balance of convenience.
Interim Injunctions.summary_line: AMERICAN CYANAMID · SERIOUS QUESTION · DAMAGES INADEQUATE (GOODWILL LOSS) · BALANCE OF CONVENIENCE · CROSS-UNDERTAKING · PROMPT APPLICATION

Interim Injunctions.front: A newspaper is about to publish an article that a public figure believes is defamatory. The public figure applies for an interim injunction to restrain publication before trial. What is the relevant test?
Interim Injunctions.answer: The standard American Cyanamid test does not apply to applications restraining publication. Under s12(3) Human Rights Act 1998, the court must not grant such relief unless it is satisfied the applicant is likely to succeed at trial. 'Likely' means more probable than not — a higher threshold than the 'serious question to be tried' test in American Cyanamid. This reflects the tension between Art 8 ECHR (privacy/reputation) and Art 10 ECHR (freedom of expression), with s12 giving significant weight to expression. The public figure must demonstrate they will probably succeed at trial — not merely that there is an arguable case.
Interim Injunctions.summary_line: s12(3) HRA 1998 · PUBLICATION CASES · HIGHER THRESHOLD: LIKELY TO SUCCEED AT TRIAL · NOT AMERICAN CYANAMID · ART 8 v ART 10 · MORE PROBABLE THAN NOT

Interim Injunctions.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A newspaper is about to publish a story that a public figure says is defamatory and private. The public figure's barrister applies for an interim injunction to restrain publication. She argues the case easily clears the American Cyanamid threshold: there is plainly a serious question to be tried on both defamation and misuse of private information.
Interim Injunctions.issue: The Trap: The barrister correctly identifies the American Cyanamid test — and the case clearly passes the 'serious question to be tried' threshold on both claims. Students who know American Cyanamid will assess the test, conclude it is passed, and proceed to consider adequacy of damages and balance of convenience.
Interim Injunctions.rule: The Reality: American Cyanamid does not apply to applications for interim injunctions to restrain publication. Section 12(3) Human Rights Act 1998 provides that where a court is considering whether to grant relief that might affect the exercise of the Convention right to freedom of expression (Art 10), no such relief is to be granted unless the court is satisfied the applicant is likely to succeed at trial. 'Likely' means more probable than not — a significantly higher threshold than 'serious question to be tried'.
Interim Injunctions.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is the American Cyanamid test, which governs most interim injunction applications and which students correctly identify. But s.12(3) HRA 1998 expressly modifies the test where freedom of expression is engaged and the respondent is not present. The higher threshold — likely to establish at trial — replaces the balance of convenience stage.
Interim Injunctions.conclusion: The Key Rule: Section 12(3) HRA 1998 disapplies American Cyanamid in cases where the injunction would restrict publication. The threshold is elevated from 'serious question to be tried' to 'likely to succeed at trial' (more probable than not). Always check whether the injunction would restrain publication before applying the standard American Cyanamid test.
Interim Injunctions.summary_line: s12(3) HRA 1998 · PUBLICATION CASES · LIKELY TO SUCCEED AT TRIAL · NOT AMERICAN CYANAMID · MORE PROBABLE THAN NOT · ART 10 FREEDOM OF EXPRESSION

Freezing Injunctions.front: What are the requirements for obtaining a freezing injunction?
Freezing Injunctions.summary_line: FREEZING INJUNCTION · GOOD ARGUABLE CASE · RISK OF DISSIPATION · WITHOUT NOTICE · s37 SCA 1981 · NO PRIORITY OVER CREDITORS

Freezing Injunctions.front: Mareva Compania Naviera SA v International Bulkcarriers SA [1975] 2 Lloyd's Rep 509
Freezing Injunctions.issue: Whether the court had jurisdiction to restrain a defendant from dissipating assets before judgment, to prevent frustration of any eventual judgment.
Freezing Injunctions.rule: The court has inherent jurisdiction (now confirmed in s37 Senior Courts Act 1981) to restrain a defendant from removing assets from the jurisdiction or otherwise dissipating them where there is a real risk that the defendant will do so before judgment can be obtained and enforced. The applicant must show: a good arguable case on the merits; assets within the jurisdiction; and a real risk of dissipation. A cross-undertaking in damages is required.
Freezing Injunctions.application: Shipowners owed freight money that they feared the charterers would transfer out of the jurisdiction before any judgment could be enforced. The Court of Appeal granted the injunction, recognising a new form of equitable relief to prevent the abuse of transferring assets to defeat creditors.
Freezing Injunctions.conclusion:
The founding authority for the freezing injunction (originally called the Mareva injunction). Now extensively codified in CPR 25 and the standard Commercial Court and Queen's Bench forms.

⚠️ Critical limit: a freezing injunction does not create security or give the applicant priority over other creditors in insolvency — it merely prevents dissipation. The standard form order includes a living expenses carve-out and permits ordinary course of business dealings.
Freezing Injunctions.summary_line: FREEZING INJUNCTION · MAREVA · REAL RISK OF DISSIPATION · GOOD ARGUABLE CASE · s37 SCA 1981 · NO PRIORITY OVER CREDITORS

Freezing Injunctions.front: Ninemia Maritime Corporation v Trave Schiffahrtsgesellschaft GmbH (The Niedersachsen) [1983] 1 WLR 1412
Freezing Injunctions.issue: What evidence must an applicant produce to establish a 'real risk of dissipation' when seeking a freezing injunction?
Freezing Injunctions.rule: The applicant must show a real risk that assets will be dissipated or dealt with in a way that would frustrate judgment — not merely that the defendant is a foreign company, or that the defendant is unlikely to pay voluntarily, or that assets are held abroad. The risk must be real (not fanciful), assessed on all the evidence. Relevant factors include: the nature of the defendant's business and assets; evidence of dishonesty, irresponsibility, or evasion in the defendant's financial dealings; and the overall picture presented by the evidence.
Freezing Injunctions.application: Shipowners applied for a Mareva injunction. The defendant was a German company with assets in Germany. The Court of Appeal held that the mere fact of being a foreign corporation with foreign assets was insufficient, without specific evidence pointing to a genuine risk of dissipation.
Freezing Injunctions.conclusion:
Defined the 'real risk' element of the freezing injunction test. The standard requires applicants to point to specific conduct or circumstances — being foreign, having foreign assets, or simply being unlikely to pay, is not enough. Evidence of asset-stripping, dishonest dealing, or deliberate evasion will usually be required.

⚠️ Link to Mareva (FLK1-DR-CASE-004): establishes the second limb of the freezing injunction test in more detail.
Freezing Injunctions.summary_line: REAL RISK OF DISSIPATION · SPECIFIC EVIDENCE REQUIRED · FOREIGN DEFENDANT INSUFFICIENT ALONE · DISHONESTY OR EVASION RELEVANT · NIEDERSACHSEN

Freezing Injunctions.front: Marcus is owed £250,000 under a contract. The defendant has just sold its main UK property and transferred the proceeds to an overseas account. Marcus wants to prevent the defendant dealing with the overseas funds. Advise on the appropriate order and requirements.
Freezing Injunctions.answer:
Marcus should apply for a worldwide freezing order (WFO).

**Requirements:**

1. Good arguable case on the merits
2. Real risk of dissipation — the sale of the UK property and transfer overseas is strong evidence
3. For a WFO, additional requirements are assets abroad and that it would be just and convenient.

The application should be made without notice given the risk of further dissipation if the defendant is alerted. Marcus must give a cross-undertaking in damages.

⚠️ A WFO does not prevent the defendant dealing with assets entirely — the standard form includes a living expenses carve-out and allows ordinary business dealings. It does not create security or priority over other creditors.
Freezing Injunctions.summary_line: WORLDWIDE FREEZING ORDER · GOOD ARGUABLE CASE · REAL RISK: ASSET SALE AND TRANSFER OVERSEAS · WITHOUT NOTICE · CROSS-UNDERTAKING · NO SECURITY · CARVE-OUTS

Freezing Injunctions.front: Going deeper: What are the limits of worldwide freezing orders, and how do English courts balance effective asset protection against jurisdictional overreach?
Freezing Injunctions.answer:
The English courts developed the worldwide freezing order (WFO) through Derby & Co Ltd v Weldon [1990] and Republic of Haiti v Duvalier [1990], driven by recognition that corporate defendants can hold assets in multiple jurisdictions.

Key tensions identified by Millett LJ in Derby v Weldon:

1. jurisdictional overreach — an English order purporting to bind overseas third parties (e.g. foreign banks) raises sovereignty concerns
2. the carve-out problem — living expenses and ordinary business carve-outs in the standard form mean a sophisticated defendant can drain assets slowly within permitted limits
3. enforcement — a WFO is only as effective as the courts of the foreign jurisdiction are willing to recognise it.

Academic commentary (notably Raphael QC, 'The Anti-Suit Injunction') identifies tension between English willingness to grant WFOs and principles of international comity. Civil law courts (Germany, France) have historically been reluctant to grant or enforce similar orders. Brexit has complicated enforcement prospects formerly available under the Lugano Convention.

Dadourian Group International v Simms [2006] (Lord Collins) provides the test for permission to enforce WFOs abroad — the Dadourian guidelines balance effectiveness against comity.

⚠️ Not SQE-tested: SQE covers the domestic freezing injunction test; WFO jurisprudence is beyond the syllabus.

Search Orders.front: What is a search order and what are the conditions for granting one?
Search Orders.summary_line: SEARCH ORDER · ANTON PILLER · EXTREMELY STRONG CASE · RISK OF DESTRUCTION · WITHOUT NOTICE · SUPERVISING SOLICITOR

Search Orders.front: Anton Piller KG v Manufacturing Processes Ltd [1976] Ch 55
Search Orders.issue: Whether the court had jurisdiction to order a defendant to permit entry to its premises for inspection and seizure of documents without prior notice, and what conditions must be satisfied.
Search Orders.rule:
The court has jurisdiction to make such an order (now CPR 25.1(1)(h)) where:

1. the applicant has an extremely strong prima facie case on the merits
2. the actual or potential damage to the applicant is very serious; and
3. there is clear evidence that the defendant possesses the relevant documents or items and there is a real possibility that they will destroy or conceal them if given notice.

Applications are always without notice. A supervising solicitor (independent of the applicant's firm) must attend the execution. The order does not permit physical force — it requires the defendant to permit entry, with contempt of court as the sanction for refusal.
Search Orders.application: A German company discovered that its English distributor was secretly supplying its trade secrets to competitors. The Court of Appeal held the court had power to order the distributor to permit inspection of its premises and seizure of relevant materials — the power to grant injunctions under s45 Supreme Court Act extended this far.
Search Orders.conclusion: The founding authority for the search order (originally called the Anton Piller order). Now codified in CPR 25. The safeguards built into the procedure (supervising solicitor, right to seek legal advice, record of items seized) reflect the serious intrusion involved. The threshold is deliberately high — 'extremely strong' prima facie case — reflecting the draconian nature of the order.
Search Orders.summary_line: SEARCH ORDER · ANTON PILLER · EXTREMELY STRONG PRIMA FACIE CASE · WITHOUT NOTICE · SUPERVISING SOLICITOR · CONTEMPT IF REFUSAL · CPR 25.1(1)(H)

Search Orders.front: A software company believes a former employee has copied proprietary source code onto a USB drive. If given notice, the employee would almost certainly destroy it. The company wants immediate access to the employee's home computer. Advise.
Search Orders.answer:
The company should apply for a search order under CPR 25.1(1)(h).

**Requirements:**

1. Extremely strong prima facie case — strong evidence the code was taken
2. Very serious actual or potential damage — loss of proprietary code could destroy the company's competitive advantage
3. Clear evidence the employee has the code and would destroy it if given notice.

All three conditions appear satisfied. The application must be made without notice. A supervising solicitor (independent of the company's firm) must attend execution. The employee is entitled to seek legal advice before permitting entry. The order does not authorise forced entry — refusal is sanctioned by contempt.
Search Orders.summary_line: SEARCH ORDER · ANTON PILLER · EXTREMELY STRONG CASE · RISK OF DESTRUCTION · WITHOUT NOTICE · SUPERVISING SOLICITOR · NO FORCED ENTRY · LEGAL ADVICE RIGHT

Without Notice Applications.front: When may an interim application be made without notice, and what duty does the applicant owe?
Without Notice Applications.answer:
A without notice application may be made where (CPR 25.3):

1. giving notice would defeat the purpose of the application (e.g. risk of asset dissipation or document destruction)
2. the matter is genuinely urgent; or
3. there are other compelling reasons.

The applicant owes a strict duty of full and frank disclosure — they must disclose all material facts, including those adverse to their case. The duty is not limited to what the applicant considers important; it covers anything a court would want to know.

Failure to make full and frank disclosure is an independent ground for the court to discharge the order, even if the applicant would otherwise have been entitled to it. The respondent may apply to set aside the order at a return date (usually fixed at the time the without notice order is made).

⚠️ Non-disclosure of a material fact can lead to discharge even if the applicant had a strong case — the duty is strict.
Without Notice Applications.summary_line: WITHOUT NOTICE · CPR 25.3 · URGENCY · DEFEAT PURPOSE · FULL AND FRANK DISCLOSURE · RETURN DATE · DISCHARGE FOR NON-DISCLOSURE

Without Notice Applications.front: In a without notice application for a freezing injunction, the claimant's solicitor fails to mention that the defendant had voluntarily offered to preserve assets in correspondence three weeks earlier. The injunction is granted. The defendant seeks discharge. Advise.
Without Notice Applications.answer: The defendant has strong grounds to have the injunction discharged for failure to make full and frank disclosure. On a without notice application, the applicant owes a strict duty to disclose all material facts, including those adverse to the application. The defendant's voluntary offer to preserve assets was directly relevant to the 'real risk of dissipation' requirement. If the court had known of this offer, it may have concluded the risk was lower. Non-disclosure of a material fact is an independent ground for discharge — even if the applicant would otherwise have been entitled to the order. Inadvertent non-disclosure is no excuse.
Without Notice Applications.summary_line: WITHOUT NOTICE · FULL AND FRANK DISCLOSURE · VOLUNTARY OFFER TO PRESERVE = MATERIAL FACT · DISCHARGE ON NON-DISCLOSURE · INDEPENDENT GROUND · INADVERTENT NON-DISCLOSURE STILL FATAL

Standards on Applications.front: What are the different standards of proof or prospect applied across civil procedure applications and how do they differ?
Standards on Applications.answer:
Different applications apply different standards:

1. Summary judgment (CPR 24.2): no real prospect of success — the claim/defence is not fanciful. Evidence is considered; no mini-trial.

2. Interim injunction (American Cyanamid): serious question to be tried — lower than prima facie case; the claim must not be frivolous or vexatious.

3. Freezing injunction: good arguable case — higher than American Cyanamid; more than a serious question but less than the balance of probabilities.

4. Search order: extremely strong prima facie case — the highest threshold on an interim application.

5. Permission to appeal (CPR 52.3): real prospect of success (first appeal) or important point of principle / compelling reason (second appeal).

6. Pre-action disclosure (CPR 31.16): respondent and applicant are likely to be parties and disclosure is desirable to fairly dispose of proceedings or save costs.

⚠️ The ascending order of threshold is: serious question (interim injunction) → good arguable case (freezing) → extremely strong prima facie case (search order). Applying the wrong standard to the wrong application type is a frequent exam error.
Standards on Applications.summary_line: STANDARDS ON APPLICATIONS · SERIOUS QUESTION (INTERIM INJ) · GOOD ARGUABLE CASE (FREEZING) · EXTREMELY STRONG (SEARCH ORDER) · REAL PROSPECT (SJ/APPEAL)

Interim Payments.front: What is an interim payment order, what are the three grounds on which it may be made, and how is the amount calculated?
Interim Payments.answer:
An interim payment order (CPR 25.6–25.9) requires the defendant to pay a sum of money to the claimant before final judgment. The court may make an order only on one of three grounds:

1. The defendant has admitted liability to pay damages;

2. The claimant has obtained judgment on liability with quantum to be assessed; or

3. The court is satisfied that, if the claim went to trial, the claimant would obtain judgment for a substantial amount of money against the defendant (or, where there are multiple defendants, against at least one of them).

Amount: the court does not order more than a reasonable proportion of the likely final award, taking into account any contributory negligence and any set-off or counterclaim (CPR 25.8).

⚠️ An interim payment order does not constitute an admission of liability by the defendant. Where a defendant's insurer or the MIB may be paying, this is relevant to the defendant's ability to pay and does not affect the court's power to make the order.

⚠️ If the final award is less than the interim payment, the court adjusts at the end of proceedings — the claimant may have to repay the excess.
Interim Payments.summary_line: INTERIM PAYMENT · CPR 25.6-25.9 · THREE GROUNDS: ADMISSION · JUDGMENT ON LIABILITY · SUBSTANTIAL DAMAGES LIKELY · REASONABLE PROPORTION · NO ADMISSION OF LIABILITY · REPAY IF OVER-AWARDED

Summary Judgment.front: What is the test for summary judgment and who may apply?
Summary Judgment.answer:
Under CPR 24.2, the court may give summary judgment against a claimant or defendant where the party has no real prospect of succeeding on the claim or defence (as applicable) and there is no other compelling reason why the case should be disposed of at trial.

Either party may apply. The test requires a 'real' (not fanciful) prospect — the court does not conduct a mini-trial or assess the credibility of witnesses; it considers the evidence on the assumption most favourable to the respondent.

The application may be made by the claimant after the defendant has filed an AoS or defence, or at any time by the defendant.

⚠️ Summary judgment (on the evidence) is distinct from strike out (on the pleadings).

⚠️ Link — Strike out (see FLK1-DR-038): strike out applies to the pleaded case without recourse to evidence; summary judgment looks beyond the pleadings.
Summary Judgment.summary_line: SUMMARY JUDGMENT · CPR 24.2 · NO REAL PROSPECT · NOT A MINI-TRIAL · CLAIMANT OR DEFENDANT · COMPELLING REASON EXCEPTION

Summary Judgment.issue: What is the correct interpretation of 'no real prospect of succeeding' for the purposes of summary judgment under CPR 24, and what approach should the court take?
Summary Judgment.rule: The word 'real' in CPR 24.2 distinguishes a prospect of success that is genuine from one that is merely fanciful. A real prospect need not be probable — it simply must not be entirely without substance. The court must not conduct a mini-trial of disputed facts at the summary judgment stage; if there is a genuine dispute of fact that can only be resolved by hearing evidence, summary judgment should be refused. The court takes the respondent's case at its highest and considers whether, even on that basis, there is any real prospect of success.
Summary Judgment.application: An application for summary judgment in a personal injury claim. The Court of Appeal confirmed the correct approach to CPR 24 and in particular emphasised that summary judgment is not the occasion for mini-trials — the procedure is designed to dispose of cases where, even accepting the respondent's evidence, the claim or defence plainly cannot succeed.
Summary Judgment.conclusion:
The principal authority on the operation of CPR 24. Establishes that summary judgment is available only where the claim or defence is plainly hopeless — not merely weak or improbable. Applied in conjunction with Three Rivers DC v Bank of England (No 3) [2001] which confirmed the same principle in the context of a complex case.

⚠️ Link: the same 'real prospect of success' language appears in the permission to appeal test (CPR 52.3) and the setting aside of default judgment test (CPR 13) — the word 'real' carries the same meaning across all three.
Summary Judgment.summary_line: SUMMARY JUDGMENT · CPR 24 · REAL PROSPECT · NOT FANCIFUL · NO MINI-TRIAL · RESPONDENT'S CASE AT HIGHEST · GENUINE DISPUTE OF FACT

Summary Judgment.front: The defendant applies for summary judgment, arguing the claimant's witness statement is contradicted by contemporaneous documents. The claimant says this is a factual dispute that must go to trial. Advise.
Summary Judgment.answer: The court will not grant summary judgment by conducting a mini-trial to resolve disputes of fact. The test under CPR 24.2 is whether the claimant has a real prospect of success, taking the claimant's case at its highest. Where there is a genuine dispute of fact between a witness statement and contemporaneous documents, the court will need to assess credibility — a function for trial, not summary judgment. Contradictions between a witness statement and documents are common and do not automatically deprive a claimant of a real prospect of success. Summary judgment is likely to be refused; the case will proceed to trial.
Summary Judgment.summary_line: SUMMARY JUDGMENT · CPR 24.2 · NO MINI-TRIAL · GENUINE FACTUAL DISPUTE → TRIAL · CREDIBILITY FOR JUDGE · REAL PROSPECT: CASE AT HIGHEST

Summary Judgment.front: The claimant has a strong case on the documents. The defendant argues at the summary judgment hearing that one disputed issue of expert valuation can only be resolved at trial. Should summary judgment be granted?
Summary Judgment.answer: The court must consider whether the disputed valuation issue gives the defendant a real prospect of success. If the valuation is central to the outcome and an expert could reasonably give evidence supporting the defendant's position, the court should refuse summary judgment — this is a genuine triable issue requiring expert evidence. Summary judgment is not appropriate where there is a genuine dispute requiring expert testimony. However, if the valuation point is peripheral or the range of possible expert opinion is so narrow no expert could reasonably support the defendant's position, the court may still grant it. The mere assertion that expert evidence is needed does not automatically defeat a summary judgment application.
Summary Judgment.summary_line: SUMMARY JUDGMENT · GENUINE TRIABLE ISSUE · EXPERT VALUATION · REAL PROSPECT IF EXPERT COULD SUPPORT DEFENDANT · PERIPHERAL ISSUE MAY NOT DEFEAT · NOT FANCIFUL

Summary Judgment.front: What does 'no real prospect of succeeding' mean for summary judgment under CPR 24.2?
Summary Judgment.answer:
The test for summary judgment under CPR 24.2 is whether the claim or defence has a **real** (as opposed to **fanciful**) prospect of success.

**Key principles from Swain v Hillman [2001]:**
- A prospect is 'real' if it is more than fanciful but need not be 'likely to succeed' — it need not pass the balance of probabilities threshold.
- The court does **not conduct a mini-trial** — it does not resolve disputed questions of fact or assess the weight of conflicting evidence.
- The court takes the **respondent's factual case at its highest** — the court assumes the best reasonably arguable version of the respondent's facts.
- A case is suitable for summary judgment where, even on the respondent's best case, there is no viable legal claim or defence.

**The threshold:** Higher than 'arguable' but lower than 'probable'. The question is whether the case has any real substance or is merely speculative.
Summary Judgment.summary_line: CPR 24.2 TEST · REAL NOT FANCIFUL · SWAIN V HILLMAN · NO MINI-TRIAL · RESPONDENT'S CASE AT HIGHEST · NOT BALANCE OF PROBABILITIES

Summary Judgment.front: Who can apply for summary judgment under CPR 24 and at what stage can they do so?
Summary Judgment.answer:
**Who can apply:**
- Either the **claimant** or the **defendant** may apply for summary judgment.
- The claimant applies to have the whole claim or part of it determined in their favour.
- The defendant applies to dismiss the whole claim or part of it.
- The **court itself** cannot ordinarily grant summary judgment of its own motion (unlike strike out, where the court has a limited power to act of its own motion in certain circumstances).

**Timing:**
- A claimant may apply after the defendant has **filed an acknowledgment of service or defence**.
- A defendant may apply **after filing an acknowledgment of service** and before or after filing a defence.
- The court may grant summary judgment at any stage in the proceedings — but the application should be made promptly, typically **before the case management conference**.

**CPR 24.3:** The court may give summary judgment against a claimant or defendant on the whole claim, part of a claim, or on a particular issue.
Summary Judgment.summary_line: EITHER PARTY CAN APPLY · NOT COURT OWN MOTION · AFTER ACKNOWLEDGMENT OR DEFENCE · PROMPTLY BEFORE CMC · CPR 24.3

Summary Judgment.front: What are conditional orders under CPR 24 and when does the court impose them instead of giving summary judgment?
Summary Judgment.answer:
A **conditional order** under CPR 24.6 is an order that requires a party to comply with a condition — typically paying a sum of money into court or providing security — before being permitted to proceed with their claim or defence.

**When used:** The court may make a conditional order (rather than giving full summary judgment) where:
- The claim or defence is **improbable or ill-founded** but not wholly hopeless — there is a real (if weak) prospect of success.
- The court is not prepared to give full summary judgment but wants to protect against an unmeritorious case proceeding without some security.

**Effect:** If the party complies with the condition, the case proceeds. If they fail to comply, the claim or defence is struck out or judgment is entered for the other side.

**Purpose:** Balances access to justice (not removing a case outright if it has some merit) against protecting against weak or speculative claims proceeding without check.
Summary Judgment.summary_line: CONDITIONAL ORDER · CPR 24.6 · PAY INTO COURT · IMPROBABLE BUT NOT HOPELESS · SECURITY FOR WEAK CASE · STRIKE OUT IF NON-COMPLIANCE

Summary Judgment.front: Three Rivers DC v Bank of England (No.3) [2003] 2 AC 1
Summary Judgment.issue: What is the correct standard of scrutiny when a court assesses whether a claim has 'no real prospect of success' for the purposes of summary judgment under CPR 24.2?
Summary Judgment.rule: The court must take the claimant's factual case at its highest — accepting all well-pleaded facts in the particulars of claim as if they were proven. The question is not whether the claimant is likely to succeed, but whether there is a real (non-fanciful) prospect of success. The court does not resolve disputed questions of fact, assess credibility, or conduct a mini-trial.
Summary Judgment.application: The House of Lords emphasised that on a summary judgment application, the respondent's factual case (as pleaded) is taken as established. The applicant must show that even on that basis, the claim cannot succeed in law. The court will not examine the weight of evidence or prefer one version of events over another — that is the function of a trial.
Summary Judgment.conclusion: Three Rivers (No.3) confirmed that 'no real prospect of success' is applied by taking the respondent's case at its highest and asking whether, on that footing, the law permits the claim to succeed. It sets a high threshold for the applicant — the case must be truly unsustainable in law, not merely improbable in fact.
Summary Judgment.summary_line: THREE RIVERS NO.3 · RESPONDENT'S CASE AT HIGHEST · NO MINI-TRIAL · HIGH THRESHOLD FOR APPLICANT · UNSUSTAINABLE IN LAW NOT IMPROBABLE IN FACT

Summary Judgment.front: A defendant argues both that (a) the claimant's particulars of claim disclose no reasonable cause of action in law, and (b) even if the claim is legally sound, there is no real prospect of the claimant succeeding on the facts. What application(s) should the defendant pursue?
Summary Judgment.answer:
The defendant should pursue **two separate applications**:

**Application 1 — Strike out under CPR 3.4(2)(a):**
Targets argument (a) — the pleadings themselves. This is a point of law: does the particulars of claim, taken at face value, disclose a recognisable cause of action? The court looks only at the document, not at evidence.

**Application 2 — Summary judgment under CPR 24.2:**
Targets argument (b) — the factual merits. The court reviews the evidence to determine whether, even taking the claimant's facts at their highest, there is no real (non-fanciful) prospect of success.

**The distinction:**
- Strike out: 'Even if everything the claimant says is true, the law gives no remedy.' (Legal question.)
- Summary judgment: 'The claimant cannot realistically prove their factual case.' (Factual/evidential question.)

**Practical approach:** Make both applications in the alternative. If the claim is legally defective, strike out is quicker and cheaper. If the legal framework is sound but the evidence is weak, summary judgment is the appropriate route.
Summary Judgment.summary_line: STRIKE OUT FOR LEGAL DEFECT · SUMMARY JUDGMENT FOR FACTUAL WEAKNESS · TWO SEPARATE APPLICATIONS · IN THE ALTERNATIVE · PLEADINGS VS EVIDENCE

Strike Out.front: On what grounds may a statement of case be struck out?
Strike Out.summary_line: STRIKE OUT · CPR 3.4(2) · NO REASONABLE GROUNDS · ABUSE OF PROCESS · NON-COMPLIANCE · PLEADINGS ONLY · DISTINGUISH SJ

Strike Out.front: The claimant's particulars of claim allege that the defendant 'acted badly and has caused loss'. No facts, legal basis, or quantification of loss are pleaded. The defendant applies to strike out. Advise.
Strike Out.answer: The particulars disclose no reasonable grounds for bringing the claim and should be struck out under CPR 3.4(2)(a). Particulars of claim must contain a concise statement of the facts relied on and must disclose a legally recognisable cause of action (CPR 16.4). 'Acted badly and caused loss' does not identify what the defendant did, why it was wrongful, what cause of action is pursued, or how loss was caused and quantified. Even taking the pleaded facts at their highest, there is no recognisable claim. The claimant should be given an opportunity to amend — if they cannot plead a proper cause of action, the claim will be struck out permanently.
Strike Out.summary_line: STRIKE OUT · CPR 3.4(2)(a) · NO REASONABLE GROUNDS · PARTICULARS INADEQUATE · NO CAUSE OF ACTION · AMENDMENT OPPORTUNITY · CPR 16.4

Strike Out.front: What are the four grounds for strike out under CPR 3.4 and how do they differ from summary judgment?
Strike Out.answer:
**CPR 3.4 — Grounds for strike out:**
1. **No reasonable grounds for bringing or defending the claim** — the pleading on its face discloses no recognisable cause of action or defence in law.
2. **Abuse of process** or conduct likely to obstruct the just disposal of proceedings — e.g. vexatious claims, re-litigation, harassment.
3. **Non-compliance** with a rule, practice direction, or court order — e.g. failure to file particulars of claim by a deadline.
4. **Failure to comply with pre-action protocol** (less commonly relied on as a standalone ground).

**How strike out differs from summary judgment:**
- **Strike out (CPR 3.4):** Concerned with the **pleadings** — whether the claim or defence has a legal basis on its face. It is a point of **law**, not fact.
- **Summary judgment (CPR 24):** Concerned with the **evidence** — whether, on the facts and law, there is a real prospect of success. It requires an assessment of the factual merits.
- A case may be legally sound but factually hopeless (summary judgment); or factually colourful but legally misconceived (strike out).
Strike Out.summary_line: STRIKE OUT CPR 3.4 · NO REASONABLE GROUNDS · ABUSE OF PROCESS · NON-COMPLIANCE · LEGAL QUESTION · DIFFERS FROM SUMMARY JUDGMENT FACTUAL MERITS

Strike Out.front: What conduct constitutes 'abuse of process' for the purposes of strike out under CPR 3.4(2)(b)?
Strike Out.answer:
Abuse of process under CPR 3.4(2)(b) covers conduct that is so improper that allowing the proceedings to continue would be an affront to justice. Key examples:

1. **Re-litigation** — bringing a claim that has already been decided between the same parties (*Henderson v Henderson* rule; cause of action estoppel; issue estoppel).
2. **Collateral attacks** on previous decisions — challenging findings of fact or law that should have been raised in earlier proceedings.
3. **Vexatious claims** — proceedings brought for the purpose of harassment or oppression, not to vindicate a legal right.
4. **Deliberate manipulation** of procedural steps — e.g. engineering delay, pursuing unfounded applications.
5. **Claims brought in bad faith** — where the claimant knows the claim has no merit.

**The test:** The court asks whether it is an abuse of the court's process to allow the claim or defence to continue. This is a high bar — courts are reluctant to shut out even arguable cases.
Strike Out.summary_line: ABUSE OF PROCESS CPR 3.4(2)(b) · RE-LITIGATION · HENDERSON V HENDERSON · VEXATIOUS CLAIMS · COLLATERAL ATTACKS · HIGH BAR

Trap cards.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A student says: 'Strike out and summary judgment are basically interchangeable — they both remove weak claims before trial, and the same test applies to both.'
Trap cards.issue: The Trap: Strike out and summary judgment are the same procedure applied to the same test — 'no real prospect of success' — because they both result in dismissal before trial.
Trap cards.rule: The Reality: Strike out (CPR 3.4) and summary judgment (CPR 24.2) are **distinct remedies with different tests and different focuses**. Strike out is concerned with whether the **pleadings** disclose a legally viable claim or defence — it is a question of law assessed from the face of the documents. Summary judgment is concerned with whether there is a **real prospect of succeeding on the merits** — it requires an assessment of evidence and facts. The 'no real prospect' language appears in CPR 24.2 only, not in CPR 3.4.
Trap cards.application: Why the Distractor Fails: Because both procedures can end a case before trial, students conflate them. The key conceptual distinction is: strike out asks 'does this claim have a legal basis at all?'; summary judgment asks 'can this claim succeed on the evidence?'. A case can be legally well-founded but evidentially hopeless (suitable for summary judgment), or legally misconceived even on strong facts (suitable for strike out). The procedures are not interchangeable.
Trap cards.conclusion: The Key Rule: Strike out = legal sufficiency of pleadings (CPR 3.4); Summary judgment = factual and evidential merits (CPR 24.2). Different tests, different grounds, different remedies. Always identify which applies to your specific argument.
Trap cards.summary_line: STRIKE OUT ≠ SUMMARY JUDGMENT · CPR 3.4 VS CPR 24.2 · LEGAL SUFFICIENCY VS FACTUAL MERITS · DIFFERENT TESTS · NOT INTERCHANGEABLE

Formal Requirements.front: What are the formal requirements for a valid Part 36 offer?
Formal Requirements.summary_line: PART 36 OFFER · WRITING · STATES PART 36 · 21-DAY RELEVANT PERIOD · WHOLE OR PART · WITHOUT PREJUDICE SAVE AS TO COSTS · CPR 36.5

Formal Requirements.front: George makes a written offer 'in full and final settlement for £30,000, without prejudice'. He does not mention Part 36. The defendant wins at trial. George argues Part 36 costs consequences should apply. Advise.
Formal Requirements.answer:
George's offer is not a valid Part 36 offer. Under CPR 36.5, a Part 36 offer must:

1. be in writing
2. state it is made pursuant to Part 36
3. specify a relevant period of not less than 21 days
4. state whether it relates to the whole or part of the claim; and
5. state whether it takes into account any counterclaim. George's offer fails at least requirements (2), (3), and probably
4. and (5).

The automatic CPR 36.17 consequences do not apply. However, the offer may still be relevant to the court's general costs discretion under CPR 44.2 — a party ignoring a reasonable settlement offer may face a costs order.
Formal Requirements.summary_line: PART 36 · FORMAL REQUIREMENTS · NOT COMPLIANT: NO PART 36 STATED · NO RELEVANT PERIOD · CPR 36.5 · NOT VALID PART 36 OFFER · CPR 44.2 GENERAL DISCRETION STILL APPLIES

Costs Consequences.front: What are the costs consequences where a claimant fails to obtain judgment more advantageous than the defendant's Part 36 offer?
Costs Consequences.answer:
Under CPR 36.17(3), where judgment against the defendant is not more advantageous to the claimant than the defendant's Part 36 offer, the court will, unless it considers it unjust, order that the defendant is entitled to:

1. Costs from the date on which the relevant period expired;

2. Interest on those costs.

The claimant retains their own costs up to the date the relevant period expired (a split costs order).

⚠️ This is asymmetrical — compare the enhanced consequences where a claimant beats their own offer (CPR 36.17(4)). The consequences for failing to beat a defendant's offer are less severe than the consequences for a defendant who fails to beat a claimant's offer.

⚠️ Link — Claimant beats own Part 36 offer (see FLK1-DR-041): the consequences in CPR 36.17
4. are more substantial and include indemnity costs and an additional amount.
Costs Consequences.summary_line: PART 36 · CLAIMANT FAILS TO BEAT DEFENDANT'S OFFER · CPR 36.17(3) · DEFENDANT'S COSTS FROM EXPIRY · SPLIT COSTS ORDER

Costs Consequences.front: What are the consequences where a claimant obtains judgment at least as advantageous as their own Part 36 offer?
Costs Consequences.summary_line: PART 36 · CLAIMANT BEATS OWN OFFER · CPR 36.17(4) · ENHANCED INTEREST · INDEMNITY COSTS · 10% ADDITIONAL AMOUNT · £75,000 CAP

Costs Consequences.front: What happens when a Part 36 offer is accepted, and what are the costs consequences?
Costs Consequences.answer:
If a Part 36 offer is accepted within the relevant period (CPR 36.13):

1. the proceedings are stayed automatically on the terms of the offer; and
2. the defendant pays the claimant's costs on the standard basis up to the date of acceptance.

These consequences are automatic — no court order is required.

If accepted after the relevant period has expired, the parties may agree costs. If they cannot agree, the court decides — there is no automatic costs entitlement. The court will consider who is to blame for the delay in acceptance.

An offer may be withdrawn after the relevant period expires (or before acceptance within it, with the court's permission). Once validly withdrawn, it ceases to have Part 36 consequences.

⚠️ Acceptance after expiry of the relevant period removes the automatic costs rule — the court exercises discretion and may not award costs to either party in full.
Costs Consequences.summary_line: PART 36 ACCEPTANCE · AUTO STAY · STANDARD BASIS COSTS · WITHIN RELEVANT PERIOD · AFTER EXPIRY COURT DISCRETION · CPR 36.13

Costs Consequences.front: What are the implications of making a Part 36 offer less than 21 days before trial?
Costs Consequences.answer:
Where a Part 36 offer is made less than 21 days before the start of the trial, the relevant period does not expire before the trial begins. The offer cannot be accepted during trial without the court's permission (CPR 36.11(3)(d)).

The Part 36 costs consequences in CPR 36.17 still potentially apply if the claimant obtains judgment at least as advantageous as their own offer (or fails to beat a defendant's offer) — but the court is more likely to find it would be 'unjust' to apply the standard consequences in full given the late timing of the offer.

⚠️ A late offer may still be a relevant offer for the court's general costs discretion under CPR 44.2, even if the automatic Part 36 consequences are disapplied or reduced. The unjust exception gives the court significant flexibility near trial.
Costs Consequences.summary_line: PART 36 · LESS THAN 21 DAYS BEFORE TRIAL · RELEVANT PERIOD DOES NOT EXPIRE · NO ACCEPTANCE WITHOUT PERMISSION · UNJUST EXCEPTION

Costs Consequences.front: The claimant makes a Part 36 offer of £45,000. The defendant does not accept. At trial the claimant is awarded exactly £45,000. What are the costs consequences?
Costs Consequences.answer:
Under CPR 36.17(4), where judgment is at least as advantageous to the claimant as their own Part 36 offer, the court will, unless unjust, order:

1. interest on damages at up to 10% above base rate from expiry of the relevant period
2. indemnity costs from the date the relevant period expired
3. interest on those costs at up to 10% above base rate; and
4. an additional amount of 10% of damages, capped at £75,000 (here, £4,500).

All four consequences apply cumulatively. 'At least as advantageous' means equal or better — a judgment of exactly £45,000 satisfies this.

⚠️ The 'unjust' exception is applied narrowly — simply losing on the merits is insufficient.
Costs Consequences.summary_line: PART 36 · CPR 36.17(4) · EXACTLY £45,000 = AT LEAST AS ADVANTAGEOUS · ALL FOUR CONSEQUENCES · ADDITIONAL 10% = £4,500 · INDEMNITY COSTS FROM EXPIRY

Costs Consequences.front: The defendant makes a Part 36 offer of £60,000. The claimant rejects it. At trial the claimant is awarded £55,000. What costs order is likely?
Costs Consequences.answer:
Under CPR 36.17(3), the judgment of £55,000 is not more advantageous than the defendant's offer of £60,000. Unless unjust, the court will order a split costs order:

1. the claimant is entitled to their standard costs from the start of proceedings to the date the relevant period expired
2. the defendant is entitled to its costs from that date onwards, plus interest.

The claimant's net recovery is significantly eroded — they recover £55,000 in damages but must pay the defendant's costs from the date the offer expired.

⚠️ This asymmetry (less severe than CPR 36.17(4)) is a key comparison point.
Costs Consequences.summary_line: PART 36 · CPR 36.17(3) · £55,000 < £60,000 OFFER · NOT MORE ADVANTAGEOUS · SPLIT COSTS ORDER · DEFENDANT'S COSTS FROM EXPIRY

Costs Consequences.front: A claimant makes a Part 36 offer of £100,000 on 28 April 2026, 10 days before trial begins on 8 May 2026. The defendant does not accept. The claimant is awarded £105,000. What are the CPR 36.17(4) consequences, if any?
Costs Consequences.answer: The offer was made 10 days before trial — less than 21 days. Under CPR 36.11(3)(d), the relevant period cannot expire before the trial begins. The automatic enhanced consequences in CPR 36.17(4) still potentially apply (£105,000 is at least as advantageous as the £100,000 offer), but the court must consider whether applying them in full would be 'unjust' given the offer was made only 10 days before trial, giving minimal time to evaluate and accept. The court has significant discretion under the 'unjust' exception and may decline to apply some or all consequences. The offer will at minimum be relevant to the general costs discretion under CPR 44.2.
Costs Consequences.summary_line: PART 36 · LESS THAN 21 DAYS BEFORE TRIAL · RELEVANT PERIOD DOES NOT EXPIRE · CPR 36.17(4) MAY STILL APPLY · UNJUST EXCEPTION MORE LIKELY · COURT DISCRETION

Costs Consequences.front: Going deeper: Is the Part 36 costs regime constitutionally sound — does imposing automatic enhanced consequences improperly fetter the court's discretion on costs?
Costs Consequences.answer:
Part 36 operates as a structured exception to the court's general costs discretion under CPR 44.2. The automatic consequences in CPR 36.17(4) — indemnity costs, enhanced interest, and the additional amount — are applied unless the court finds it 'unjust' to do so. The unjust exception is deliberately narrow; courts have consistently declined to read it broadly (Gibbon v Manchester City Council [2010]; Smith v Trafford Housing Trust [2012]).

In Transformers & Rectifiers Ltd v Needs Ltd [2015], the court held that the CPR 36.17 consequences were mandatory in the absence of injustice — there is no general discretion to scale them back proportionally.

Academic commentary (Gourlay, 'Part 36 — A Constitutional Challenge?', Civil Justice Quarterly 2017) argues that the additional amount under CPR 36.17(4)(d) functions as punitive damages rather than a genuine costs sanction, and that imposing it automatically without assessment of culpability raises questions under ECHR Art 6 (right to fair hearing) and Art 1 Protocol 1 (right to property).

Lord Neuberger NPJ (extra-judicially) has suggested the court should retain greater discretion in applying Part 36 consequences, particularly the additional amount, to avoid producing windfalls disproportionate to the underlying claim.
